Front:
An enterprise AI application requires deployment across multiple regions with <99.9% uptime SLA and must support both OpenAI GPT-4 and custom vision models. The solution needs unified monitoring, key management, and endpoint configuration across all AI services.

A) Deploy Azure OpenAI and Azure AI Vision separately with individual authentication
B) Create an Azure AI Services multi-service resource
C) Use Azure Kubernetes Service to containerize all models
Back:
B) Create an Azure AI Services multi-service resource

Azure AI Services multi-service resources provide a unified endpoint, single key management, and consolidated billing/monitoring across multiple AI capabilities. Option A creates management overhead with separate endpoints. Option C adds unnecessary infrastructure complexity for managed services.

Reference: Multi-service resource overview
Extra: Multi-service resources simplify authentication (one key for all services), provide unified monitoring in Azure Monitor, and enable consolidated cost tracking. Separate resources require managing multiple endpoints, keys, and monitoring streams. Use multi-service for production deployments; use single-service only when strict cost isolation per capability is required.

Front:
A financial services company must implement content filtering for a customer-facing chatbot to prevent disclosure of sensitive financial data and PII. The system must block prompts attempting to extract customer account details while allowing legitimate banking queries. Monitoring must detect attempted prompt injection attacks.

A) Implement Azure Content Safety with custom blocklists and prompt shields
B) Use Azure API Management rate limiting and throttling
C) Configure Azure Application Gateway with WAF rules
Back:
A) Implement Azure Content Safety with custom blocklists and prompt shields

Azure Content Safety provides AI-specific protections including prompt shields (detects jailbreak attempts), content filters (blocks harmful outputs), and custom blocklists (prevents specific sensitive terms). APIM throttling (B) only limits request volume. WAF (C) protects against web attacks but lacks AI-specific threat detection like prompt injection.

Reference: Prompt shields documentation
Extra: Prompt shields detect direct and indirect attacks (user prompt injections and document-embedded attacks). Custom blocklists enable domain-specific filtering (e.g., "account number", "SSN"). Content Safety integrates with Azure OpenAI and monitors in real-time. WAF protects infrastructure but doesn't understand LLM-specific attacks like jailbreaks or adversarial prompts.

Front:
An Azure OpenAI deployment must scale to 100,000 TPM during business hours but minimize costs overnight when usage drops to 5,000 TPM. The solution requires deployment in multiple Azure regions for disaster recovery with automatic failover.

A) Deploy Provisioned Throughput Units with autoscaling
B) Use Standard deployment with Azure Front Door load balancing
C) Implement Container Instances with horizontal pod autoscaling
Back:
B) Use Standard deployment with Azure Front Door load balancing

Standard (pay-per-token) deployments automatically scale to demand without provisioning capacity and only charge for actual usage. Front Door provides multi-region routing with health probes for failover. PTU (A) requires upfront capacity commitment not suited for 20x variance. Container deployments (C) aren't applicable to Azure OpenAI managed service.

Reference: Azure OpenAI deployment types
Extra: Standard deployments suit variable workloads (pay-per-token, no upfront commitment). Provisioned Throughput Units (PTU) suit predictable high-volume workloads needing guaranteed capacity and lower per-token costs at scale. PTU minimum commitment is typically 50,000 TPM monthly. For 20x daily variance, Standard is more cost-effective; for sustained 100k+ TPM, PTU provides cost savings.

Front:
An AI application processes 10,000 document processing requests daily using Azure Document Intelligence. Cost monitoring shows the service exceeds budget due to processing high-resolution scanned documents. The solution must reduce costs while maintaining OCR accuracy for standard business documents.

A) Downscale document resolution to 150 DPI before processing
B) Switch to Azure Vision OCR API instead of Document Intelligence
C) Implement Azure AI Search with built-in OCR skillset
Back:
A) Downscale document resolution to 150 DPI before processing

Document Intelligence pricing is per page processed. Pre-processing images to 150-200 DPI reduces processing time and data transfer costs while maintaining OCR quality for printed text. Vision OCR (B) lacks document structure extraction (tables, forms) that Document Intelligence provides. AI Search OCR (C) still uses same underlying service and adds indexing costs.

Reference: Document layout analysis
Extra: Document Intelligence charges per page analyzed. Optimization strategies: (1) Downscale images to 150-200 DPI (sufficient for printed text), (2) Use Read model for simple text extraction instead of Layout model when structure isn't needed, (3) Batch processing for volume discounts. Vision OCR is cheaper but doesn't extract tables/forms/structure. Choose based on whether you need structured data extraction.

Front:
A government application must authenticate AI service calls using Azure AD managed identities without storing credentials in code or configuration files. The solution must support both Azure Functions (compute) and Azure Logic Apps (integration) calling Azure OpenAI services.

A) Configure system-assigned managed identity for each resource with Azure OpenAI RBAC role
B) Use Azure Key Vault to store API keys and reference them at runtime
C) Generate SAS tokens with limited time-to-live for each request
Back:
A) Configure system-assigned managed identity for each resource with Azure OpenAI RBAC role

System-assigned managed identities eliminate credential management entirely. Both Functions and Logic Apps support managed identities. Assign "Cognitive Services OpenAI User" role at Azure OpenAI resource scope. Key Vault (B) still requires managing secrets and retrieval logic. SAS tokens (C) aren't supported for Azure OpenAI authentication.

Reference: Managed identity with Azure OpenAI
Extra: System-assigned identities: Automatically managed lifecycle (deleted with resource), scoped to single resource. User-assigned identities: Independent lifecycle, reusable across multiple resources. For Azure OpenAI, assign "Cognitive Services OpenAI User" (inference) or "Cognitive Services OpenAI Contributor" (management) roles. Managed identities eliminate key rotation requirements and credential exposure risks.

Front:
An enterprise must implement custom content filtering for an Azure OpenAI chatbot that blocks competitor product names and allows company-specific terminology in prompts and completions. The filter must work in real-time with minimal latency impact (<100ms) and log all blocked content for compliance review.

A) Configure Azure OpenAI content filters with custom blocklists and severity thresholds
B) Implement Azure API Management policy with regex pattern matching
C) Deploy Azure Functions with Custom Content Safety evaluator in the prompt flow
Back:
A) Configure Azure OpenAI content filters with custom blocklists and severity thresholds

Azure OpenAI content filters support custom blocklists (up to 10,000 terms) with low latency (20-30ms overhead). Blocklists apply to both inputs and outputs. APIM policies (B) add latency and lack semantic understanding. Functions with evaluators (C) add significant latency and require maintaining separate deployment.

Reference: Custom blocklists for Azure OpenAI
Extra: Custom blocklists support exact match and wildcard patterns. Apply to: prompts (prevent users mentioning competitors), completions (prevent model generating competitor names), or both. Native integration adds minimal latency. For semantic filtering (concepts vs keywords), use Content Safety custom categories. Blocklists complement default content filters (hate, violence, sexual, self-harm) which continue operating.

Front:
A generative AI solution must implement RAG pattern to ground GPT-4 responses in company product documentation stored in SharePoint (500,000 pages). The system needs semantic search with hybrid ranking combining keyword and vector similarity for optimal retrieval accuracy.

A) Use Azure AI Search with semantic ranking and vector search, index SharePoint content
B) Implement Azure Cosmos DB for MongoDB vCore with vector embeddings
C) Deploy Azure Database for PostgreSQL with pgvector extension
Back:
A) Use Azure AI Search with semantic ranking and vector search, index SharePoint content

Azure AI Search provides native hybrid search (combining keyword BM25 + vector similarity), semantic ranking powered by Microsoft AI, built-in SharePoint connector, and skillsets for document cracking. Cosmos DB MongoDB (B) requires custom indexing and ranking logic. PostgreSQL pgvector (C) lacks document processing and ranking capabilities.

Reference: Azure AI Search overview
Extra: Hybrid search combines: (1) Keyword search (BM25 algorithm) for exact term matches, (2) Vector search (cosine similarity) for semantic meaning, (3) Semantic ranking for result reordering using deep learning models. Azure AI Search also provides: document cracking (PDF, Office), skillsets (custom enrichment), knowledge store (projections). For RAG, AI Search is purpose-built; vector databases require building search infrastructure.

Front:
An Azure OpenAI application generates product descriptions that must maintain consistent formatting (bullet points, character limits) and tone (professional, technical). The solution must be easily updatable by marketing team without code changes and support A/B testing different prompt variations.

A) Hardcode system prompts in application with feature flags for variations
B) Store prompt templates in Azure App Configuration with parameterization
C) Use Azure OpenAI function calling to format outputs
Back:
B) Store prompt templates in Azure App Configuration with parameterization

App Configuration supports versioning, feature flags (A/B testing), and dynamic refresh without redeploy. Prompt templates can include parameters for product-specific variables. Hardcoding (A) requires code changes and deployment. Function calling (C) is for tool integration, not prompt management.

Reference: Azure App Configuration overview
Extra: Prompt template management strategies: (1) App Configuration: versioned, feature-flagged, dynamic refresh; (2) Azure Storage: simple, requires app restart; (3) Azure Cosmos DB: queryable metadata, versioning. For production, use App Configuration for: hot-reload capability, built-in feature flags, access control. Templates should include: system message (role/behavior), few-shot examples (output format), constraints (length, style).

Front:
A healthcare AI chatbot using GPT-4 must generate responses with clinical accuracy verification. The solution needs to configure model parameters to reduce hallucinations and implement self-reflection to validate medical information before responding to patients.

A) Set temperature=0.0, implement prompt flow with validation tool that queries medical knowledge base
B) Set temperature=1.0 for creativity, use top_p=0.95 for diversity
C) Enable streaming responses with real-time human review
Back:
A) Set temperature=0.0, implement prompt flow with validation tool that queries medical knowledge base

Temperature=0.0 produces deterministic, factual responses (minimizes hallucination). Prompt flow validation tools enable model reflection by querying authoritative sources (e.g., medical KB) before final response. Temperature=1.0 (B) increases randomness/creativity (wrong for factual accuracy). Streaming with human review (C) doesn't prevent initial generation of hallucinations.

Reference: Completion parameters
Extra: Temperature controls randomness: 0.0 = deterministic (factual tasks), 1.0 = creative (ideation). Top_p (nucleus sampling): 0.1 = conservative, 1.0 = diverse. For high-accuracy scenarios: temperature=0.0-0.3, top_p=0.1-0.5. Model reflection pattern: (1) Generate draft response, (2) Use validation tool/prompt to check facts, (3) Revise or reject response. Implement in Azure Prompt Flow with custom Python tools.

Front:
An organization deploys GPT-4 for code generation in an IDE extension used by 5,000 developers. The solution must minimize latency for code completions (<500ms) while tracking token usage per developer team for chargeback and identifying performance bottlenecks in the generation pipeline.

A) Enable Azure OpenAI diagnostic settings to Log Analytics, use Application Insights for client telemetry
B) Implement custom logging in application code only
C) Use Azure Monitor workbooks without detailed logging
Back:
A) Enable Azure OpenAI diagnostic settings to Log Analytics, use Application Insights for client telemetry

Azure OpenAI diagnostic logs capture: token counts, latency, model version, prompt filter results. Log Analytics enables Kusto queries for per-team analysis. Application Insights traces end-to-end request flow (client→API→response). Option B misses service-side metrics. Option C provides visualization but lacks granular data for cost allocation.

Reference: Monitor Azure OpenAI
Extra: Key metrics to monitor: (1) PTU utilization (for provisioned deployments), (2) Token usage by dimension (team, application), (3) HTTP status codes (429 = rate limit), (4) E2E latency breakdown (network, model processing, post-processing). Use Log Analytics queries to: aggregate usage by custom dimensions, identify slow requests, forecast capacity needs. Application Insights dependency tracking shows Azure OpenAI as dependency with timing.

Front:
A multi-agent AI solution requires orchestration where a planner agent decomposes user requests, delegates to specialist agents (data analysis, report generation, email), and aggregates results. The system must handle failures gracefully and maintain conversation context across agent calls with maximum 5-minute total execution time.

A) Implement Azure Foundry Agent Service with autogen for multi-agent orchestration
B) Use Azure Logic Apps with sequential action groups
C) Deploy Azure Durable Functions with activity functions for each agent
Back:
A) Implement Azure Foundry Agent Service with autogen for multi-agent orchestration

Azure Foundry Agent Service (based on autogen framework) provides native multi-agent patterns: sequential chats, hierarchical orchestration, and group chat for agent collaboration. Supports built-in planning, tool calling, and state management. Logic Apps (B) lacks AI agent abstractions. Durable Functions (C) requires custom agent logic without built-in LLM integration.

Reference: Develop agents in Azure Foundry
Extra: Multi-agent patterns: (1) Sequential: Agents execute in order (A→B→C), (2) Hierarchical: Manager agent coordinates specialists, (3) Group chat: Agents collaborate interactively. Autogen provides: conversation patterns, built-in retry/error handling, code execution (sandboxed), human-in-loop. Use Azure Foundry for: complex workflows, tool integration, state persistence. Use single agent for: simple Q&A, content generation.

Front:
An autonomous customer support agent must handle complex multi-turn conversations, access three backend systems (CRM, inventory, ticketing) via APIs, and escalate to human agents when confidence is below 70%. The agent needs persistent memory of customer interaction history and conversation context spanning multiple sessions.

A) Create agent in Azure Foundry with custom tools for each API, use Cosmos DB for conversation state
B) Implement Azure Bot Framework with LUIS for intent recognition
C) Deploy standalone Azure OpenAI with custom application code
Back:
A) Create agent in Azure Foundry with custom tools for each API, use Cosmos DB for conversation state

Azure Foundry agents support: custom tools (Python functions wrapping APIs), conversation memory (Cosmos DB integration), and built-in confidence scoring. Bot Framework (B) lacks native LLM integration and tool orchestration. Standalone OpenAI (C) requires building agent orchestration, memory, tool execution from scratch.

Reference: Agent development in Foundry
Extra: Agent components: (1) Tools: Functions agent can call (APIs, databases, calculators), (2) Memory: Conversation history + semantic memory (vector DB for relevant past interactions), (3) Planning: Break complex tasks into steps, (4) Reflection: Evaluate actions and revise. Azure Foundry provides: tool registration, automatic function calling, state management. For confidence threshold, evaluate: retrieval scores, model confidence (logprobs), validation tool results.

Front:
An AI application must perform OCR on 50,000 scanned invoices daily, extract key-value pairs (invoice number, date, total, line items), and validate extracted data against business rules. Documents are in English, Spanish, and French with varying layouts from 200+ vendors.

A) Use Azure Document Intelligence prebuilt invoice model with custom post-processing
B) Train Azure AI Vision custom model with labeled invoice images
C) Use Azure AI Search OCR skillset with cognitive skills
Back:
A) Use Azure Document Intelligence prebuilt invoice model with custom post-processing

Document Intelligence prebuilt invoice model is trained on millions of invoices globally, extracts 30+ common fields with confidence scores, and supports multiple languages. Vision custom model (B) requires extensive training data and lacks document structure understanding. AI Search OCR (C) extracts text but doesn't understand invoice semantics (totals, line items).

Reference: Invoice model overview
Extra: Document Intelligence hierarchy: (1) Read model: Basic OCR, returns text and layout, (2) Layout model: Adds tables, selection marks, structure, (3) Prebuilt models: Domain-specific extraction (invoices, receipts, IDs, W-2s), (4) Custom models: Train on your document types. For invoices: use prebuilt model when fields match common patterns; train custom model for company-specific fields or unique layouts. Prebuilt supports 30+ field types with built-in validation.

Front:
A retail company needs real-time product recognition in their mobile app where customers photograph items to get information. The solution must identify 5,000 SKUs with >95% accuracy, work offline on-device, and update model weekly as new products are added.

A) Train Azure AI Custom Vision classification model, export to TensorFlow Lite for mobile
B) Use Azure AI Vision image analysis with custom tags
C) Implement Azure AI Video Indexer with custom models
Back:
A) Train Azure AI Custom Vision classification model, export to TensorFlow Lite for mobile

Custom Vision supports multi-class classification for thousands of SKUs, exports models to TensorFlow Lite/CoreML for offline mobile inference, and enables iterative retraining with new images. Vision API image analysis (B) requires online connectivity and doesn't support training on custom SKUs. Video Indexer (C) is for video content, not still image classification.

Reference: Custom Vision overview
Extra: Custom Vision supports: (1) Classification: Assign labels to entire image (single-label or multi-label), (2) Object detection: Locate and classify multiple objects with bounding boxes. Export formats: TensorFlow (mobile/IoT), ONNX (cross-platform), CoreML (iOS). Model size tradeoffs: Compact models (faster, mobile-optimized) vs. Standard (higher accuracy, cloud inference). Retraining strategy: Start with 30-50 images per class, add misclassified examples iteratively.

Front:
A manufacturing facility must detect when workers enter hazardous zones without proper PPE in real-time video feeds from 50 cameras. The solution needs to count people in designated areas, track movement patterns, and trigger alerts within 2 seconds for safety violations.

A) Deploy Azure Vision Spatial Analysis containers on-premises with zone definitions
B) Stream video to Azure AI Video Indexer for analysis
C) Use Azure AI Vision image analysis API on extracted frames
Back:
A) Deploy Azure Vision Spatial Analysis containers on-premises with zone definitions

Spatial Analysis provides real-time people detection, zone-based counting, and line-crossing events with low latency (<1s). On-premises containers eliminate network latency for safety-critical scenarios. Video Indexer (B) is for content analysis (faces, labels, transcripts) not real-time spatial analytics. Frame-by-frame API calls (C) add latency and cost.

Reference: Spatial Analysis overview
Extra: Spatial Analysis operations: (1) PersonCount: Count people in zone, (2) PersonCrossingLine: Directional line crossing, (3) PersonZoneCrossing: Entry/exit zones, (4) PersonDistance: Social distancing monitoring. Deploy on: IoT Edge devices with GPU (NVIDIA T4, V100) for on-premises real-time processing. Use cases: occupancy monitoring, queue analytics, safety zones. Latency: <500ms per frame. For PPE detection, combine with Custom Vision model.

Front:
A global customer service application must provide real-time speech-to-text transcription in 20 languages with custom vocabulary for industry-specific terms (product names, technical jargon). The solution needs speaker diarization to identify which agent/customer is speaking and must achieve <90ms latency for live transcription display.

A) Use Azure Speech Service with custom speech model and speaker profiles
B) Implement Azure OpenAI Whisper model for transcription
C) Deploy Azure Video Indexer audio analysis
Back:
A) Use Azure Speech Service with custom speech model and speaker profiles

Azure Speech STT supports: 100+ languages, custom speech models (train on domain vocabulary), speaker diarization (identify who spoke when), and real-time streaming with <100ms latency. Whisper (B) lacks custom vocabulary support and real-time streaming. Video Indexer (C) is for pre-recorded content analysis, not live transcription.

Reference: Speech-to-text overview
Extra: Custom Speech improves accuracy for: domain-specific vocabulary, accents, acoustic environments. Training requires: (1) Plain text (product names, jargon), (2) Audio + human-labeled transcripts (optional but improves further). Speaker diarization returns: speaker ID per segment ("Speaker 1: hello"). Real-time vs. Batch: Real-time streaming for live transcription (<100ms), Batch for pre-recorded audio (higher accuracy, slower). Use Speech SDK for streaming audio.

Front:
An e-commerce chatbot must detect customer intent, extract entities (product categories, price ranges, brands), and support multi-turn conversations with context awareness across 5 languages. The solution needs to handle 10,000 concurrent conversations with <200ms response time for intent classification.

A) Build Conversational Language Understanding (CLU) project with slot filling and multilingual training
B) Use Azure OpenAI GPT-4 function calling for intent and entity extraction
C) Implement LUIS with entity extractors
Back:
A) Build Conversational Language Understanding (CLU) project with slot filling and multilingual training

CLU is the successor to LUIS, offers: multilingual models (train once, deploy across languages), slot filling (extract entities in context), and conversation projects (multi-turn flows). Sub-200ms latency at scale. GPT-4 (B) adds latency (1-3s) and cost for simple intent classification. LUIS (C) is being deprecated in favor of CLU.

Reference: CLU overview
Extra: CLU vs. LUIS: CLU offers better multilingual support, improved entity extraction, orchestration workflows. Training: Provide utterances (sample user inputs) with labeled intents and entities. Entities types: (1) Learned: ML-extracted from context ("cheap laptop" → price_range=low, category=laptop), (2) List: Exact match synonyms, (3) Prebuilt: numbers, dates, temperature. Multi-turn: Use dialog orchestration to track conversation state and context variables.

Front:
A knowledge management system must provide accurate answers to 100,000 employee queries monthly about HR policies, IT procedures, and benefits. The solution needs to support follow-up questions, cite source documents, update content from SharePoint without retraining, and escalate to human when confidence is below 80%.

A) Create Custom Question Answering project with multi-turn conversations and active learning
B) Implement Azure OpenAI with RAG pattern using Azure AI Search
C) Deploy Azure Bot Service with QnA Maker
Back:
B) Implement Azure OpenAI with RAG pattern using Azure AI Search

RAG with OpenAI provides: natural conversational responses, source citations, dynamic content updates (no retraining), and handles follow-up questions with conversation context. Custom QA (A) provides rigid Q&A pairs (less natural). QnA Maker (C) is deprecated, migrated to Custom Question Answering which still has limitations vs. GPT+RAG for complex conversations.

Reference: RAG with Azure AI Search
Extra: Choose QA approach: (1) Custom QA: Structured FAQ, exact/similar question matching, multi-turn prompts, good for defined Q&A pairs; (2) GPT+RAG: Natural language, handles paraphrasing, complex follow-ups, generates answers from retrieved context. RAG implementation: Index documents in AI Search → User query → Retrieve relevant docs (hybrid search) → Send to GPT with context → GPT generates answer + cites sources. Confidence: Check retrieval scores + response validation.

Front:
A healthcare application must analyze patient feedback surveys for sentiment, extract medical entities (conditions, medications, symptoms), detect PII for redaction, and identify urgent emotional distress requiring immediate follow-up. The solution must process 50,000 responses daily in compliance with HIPAA.

A) Use Azure AI Language service with sentiment analysis, healthcare entities, and PII detection
B) Implement Azure OpenAI for entity extraction and sentiment analysis
C) Deploy custom BERT model trained on medical text
Back:
A) Use Azure AI Language service with sentiment analysis, healthcare entities, and PII detection

Azure AI Language provides: healthcare-specific entity recognition (medications, diagnoses, procedures), PII detection/redaction (HIPAA compliance), sentiment analysis with opinion mining, and pre-trained models. OpenAI (B) lacks specialized healthcare entity extraction and HIPAA compliance features. Custom BERT (C) requires data, compute, and ML expertise.

Reference: Azure AI Language overview
Extra: Azure AI Language capabilities: (1) Sentiment: Document/sentence-level with opinion mining targets ("the medication [target] was effective [positive]"), (2) NER: General (person, location, org) + Healthcare domain (conditions, medications, dosages), (3) PII detection: Identifies and redacts SSN, medical record numbers, dates, names. Healthcare entities include: medication names, dosages, routes (oral, IV), conditions, symptoms, procedures. Use for: compliance, clinical insights, safety monitoring.

Front:
A global content platform must translate user-generated articles from 15 languages into English while preserving technical terminology (product names, APIs, frameworks). The solution needs custom glossaries per content category, maintain document formatting, and handle 500,000 words daily with 24-hour SLA.

A) Use Azure Translator with custom models and dictionaries for domain terminology
B) Implement Azure OpenAI GPT-4 for translation with prompt engineering
C) Deploy open-source translation models in Azure ML
Back:
A) Use Azure Translator with custom models and dictionaries for domain terminology

Azure Translator provides: 100+ languages, custom models trained on parallel texts, custom dictionaries (term-by-term glossaries), document translation (preserves formatting), and high throughput. GPT-4 translation (B) is slower and more expensive for high-volume batch translation. Azure ML custom models (C) require training data, infrastructure management.

Reference: Custom Translator overview
Extra: Translation approaches: (1) Azure Translator: Fast, cost-effective, custom terminology, document translation; (2) GPT-4: Contextual awareness, creative/marketing content, lower volume; (3) Custom models: Ultimate control, requires ML expertise. Custom Translator: Train with parallel texts (source+target pairs) for domain adaptation. Custom dictionaries: Override specific terms ("Azure Functions" → don't translate). Document translation: PDF, Word, HTML with layout preservation.

Front:
A legal firm must implement semantic search across 2 million case documents enabling lawyers to find precedents by conceptual similarity, not just keywords. The solution needs result explanations showing why documents matched, filtering by jurisdiction and date, and <3 second query response time at 100 QPS.

A) Implement Azure AI Search with semantic ranking, vector search, and captions
B) Use Azure Cosmos DB with vector embeddings and custom ranking
C) Deploy Elasticsearch with BM25 ranking
Back:
A) Implement Azure AI Search with semantic ranking, vector search, and captions

Azure AI Search semantic ranking uses deep learning for conceptual matching beyond keywords, provides captions (highlighted relevant passages explaining match), supports fast filtering on structured fields (jurisdiction, date), and scales to 100+ QPS. Cosmos DB (B) requires custom semantic ranking logic. Elasticsearch (C) relies on keyword matching without semantic understanding.

Reference: Semantic search in AI Search
Extra: Semantic ranking enhances results using: (1) Query understanding: Interprets intent, (2) Document relevance: L2 reranking with semantic models, (3) Captions: Extracts relevant passage explaining match. Hybrid search combines: BM25 (keyword) + vector search (embeddings) + semantic ranking. Query flow: User query → Hybrid retrieval (BM25 + vectors) → Top 50 results → Semantic reranking → Final results with captions/answers. Enable for: conceptual search, question answering, natural language queries.

Front:
An enterprise knowledge mining solution must process 500,000 documents (PDF, Word, emails) stored in Azure Blob Storage, extract text, identify people/organizations/locations, detect language, and populate a searchable index. The pipeline must run nightly with error handling and support incremental updates without reprocessing all content.

A) Create Azure AI Search indexer with built-in cognitive skills and change detection
B) Implement Azure Data Factory with custom OCR and NLP activities
C) Use Azure Functions to process blobs and write to Cosmos DB
Back:
A) Create Azure AI Search indexer with built-in cognitive skills and change detection

AI Search indexers provide: automatic scheduling, built-in cognitive skillsets (OCR, entity recognition, key phrases, language detection), incremental indexing (tracks changes via timestamps/etags), error handling with retries. ADF (B) requires custom integration and lacks search index capabilities. Functions (C) requires building entire pipeline and index management.

Reference: Indexers in Azure AI Search
Extra: Indexer components: (1) Data source: Blob Storage, SQL, Cosmos DB with credentials, (2) Skillset: Cognitive enrichment pipeline (OCR → language → entities → key phrases), (3) Index: Schema with fields, analyzers, suggesters, (4) Indexer: Orchestration with schedule, field mappings, change detection. Change detection types: High water mark (timestamp), integrated (etag), soft delete. Skillset built-in: OCR, entity recognition, sentiment, key phrase, language, image analysis, translation.

Front:
A document processing application must extract structured data from custom forms (purchase orders with 30 fields) unique to the company's format. The solution needs 95% accuracy, handle 10,000 forms monthly with varying quality (scanned, photos), and support form template updates without extensive retraining.

A) Train Azure Document Intelligence custom model with 50 labeled samples
B) Use Document Intelligence prebuilt models with custom post-processing
C) Implement Azure Vision OCR with template matching
Back:
A) Train Azure Document Intelligence custom model with 50 labeled samples

Document Intelligence custom models learn form structure and field locations from labeled examples, achieve 95%+ accuracy with 50-100 training documents, and generalize to quality variations. Prebuilt models (B) don't support company-specific field types. Vision OCR + templates (C) are brittle to layout changes and require extensive rule engineering.

Reference: Custom models for Document Intelligence
Extra: Custom model types: (1) Custom template: Fixed layout forms (insurance forms, invoices from single vendor), (2) Custom neural: Varying layouts with same semantic fields (invoices from multiple vendors). Training: Label 5 samples minimum, 50+ for production accuracy. Compose models: Combine multiple custom models (different form types) into single endpoint that auto-classifies and routes. Labeling: Mark fields and tables in Studio, SDK auto-generates features.

Front:
A media company must analyze video content (interviews, presentations, webinars) to extract: speaker transcripts with timestamps, identify topics discussed, detect brand logos, recognize faces of executives, and generate searchable metadata. The solution must process 100 hours of video weekly and support multiple languages.

A) Use Azure AI Video Indexer with custom person and brand models
B) Implement Azure Speech Service for transcription + Azure Vision for frame analysis
C) Deploy Azure Media Services with custom AI models
Back:
A) Use Azure AI Video Indexer with custom person and brand models

Video Indexer provides integrated: multi-language transcription with speaker diarization, topic extraction, visual content recognition (faces, logos, text), emotion detection, and custom models for company-specific people/brands. Option B requires custom orchestration across services. Media Services (C) lacks AI analysis capabilities.

Reference: Video Indexer overview
Extra: Video Indexer insights: (1) Audio: Transcription, speaker identification, language detection, emotion, (2) Visual: Face recognition, OCR, logo detection, scenes/shots, (3) Content: Topics, keywords, sentiment, brands. Custom models: Train person model (upload face images) and brand model (company-specific brands). Output: JSON with timestamps for all insights. Integration: Embed player widget, use REST API for programmatic access, export to Azure AI Search for unified search.

Front:
An Azure AI Search solution must implement vector search for semantic document retrieval using embeddings from 1 million documents. The solution needs to support hybrid search combining vector similarity with keyword filtering on metadata fields (department, date_range) and requires <100ms query latency at 50 QPS.

A) Configure Azure AI Search with vector fields, HNSW algorithm, and hybrid query composition
B) Use Azure Cosmos DB for MongoDB vCore with vector indexes
C) Implement Azure Database for PostgreSQL with pgvector extension
Back:
A) Configure Azure AI Search with vector fields, HNSW algorithm, and hybrid query composition

Azure AI Search provides: native vector storage and indexing (HNSW, Graph-based), hybrid queries combining vector similarity + filters + full-text in single request, and optimized performance for sub-100ms latency. Cosmos DB vCore (B) requires custom hybrid query logic and result merging. PostgreSQL pgvector (C) lacks enterprise search features (facets, filters, analyzers).

Reference: Vector search in Azure AI Search
Extra: Vector search implementation: (1) Generate embeddings: Use Azure OpenAI text-embedding-ada-002 or text-embedding-3-large, (2) Index definition: Add vector field with HNSW or Exhaustive KNN algorithm, (3) Query: Send vector(query_embedding) + filters. HNSW: Approximate nearest neighbors, fast, scalable. Exhaustive KNN: Exact results, slower, small datasets. Hybrid queries: Combine vector similarity + full-text BM25 + filters using @search.score parameter for ranking.

Front:
A financial trading application must extract real-time sentiment and key financial entities (stock tickers, company names, monetary values) from 500,000 news articles daily in 12 languages. The solution needs to track sentiment trends over time with sub-minute latency and integrate with Azure Synapse for analytics.

A) Use Azure AI Language text analytics with entity recognition and sentiment analysis
B) Implement Azure OpenAI GPT-4 with custom entity extraction prompts

Azure AI Language provides: named entity recognition (NER) with financial entities prebuilt, sentiment analysis at document/sentence level with confidence scores, 100+ language support, and high-throughput batch processing (<1s per document). GPT-4 (B) adds latency (2-5s) and cost for large-scale extraction. Custom BERT (C) requires training data, ML infrastructure, and expertise.

Reference: NER overview
Extra: Azure AI Language NER categories: Person, Organization, Location, DateTime, Quantity, plus domain-specific (healthcare, financial). Entity linking connects entities to Wikipedia knowledge base. For financial: detects tickers, currency amounts, percentages. Sentiment returns: positive/negative/neutral/mixed with confidence scores. Opinion mining extracts target-opinion pairs ("the stock [target] fell sharply [negative]"). Batch processing: Send up to 1000 documents per request for throughput optimization.

Front:
An AI-powered document management system must automatically classify incoming documents (contracts, invoices, resumes, reports) into 50 categories, route them to appropriate teams, and extract metadata for indexing. The classifier must improve accuracy over time as users correct misclassifications with >92% accuracy target.

A) Train Azure AI Custom Text Classification with active learning enabled
B) Use Azure OpenAI GPT-4 with zero-shot classification prompts
C) Implement Azure AI Language CLU with intent classification
Back:
A) Train Azure AI Custom Text Classification with active learning enabled

Custom Text Classification supports: multi-class and multi-label classification for 50+ categories, training on labeled documents, active learning (retraining with corrected predictions), deployment at scale. GPT-4 zero-shot (B) lacks consistent accuracy for 50 classes without fine-tuning and adds cost. CLU (C) is for conversational intent/entity extraction, not document classification.

Reference: Custom Text Classification overview
Extra: Classification types: (1) Single-label: One category per document (contract XOR invoice), (2) Multi-label: Multiple categories per document (contract AND legal AND HR). Training: Requires 50+ labeled examples per class minimum, 200+ for high accuracy. Active learning loop: User corrections → retrain model → deploy updated version. Alternative: For simple categorization rules, use Azure Logic Apps with text analytics; for complex/ambiguous categories requiring reasoning, use Custom Text Classification.

Front:
A customer support AI must generate concise summaries of lengthy support tickets (up to 10,000 words) highlighting issue description, customer sentiment, requested actions, and urgency for agent triage. The solution must process 20,000 tickets daily with consistent summary structure and support extractive and abstractive summarization modes.

A) Use Azure AI Language document summarization with aspect-based extraction
B) Implement Azure OpenAI GPT-4 with custom summarization prompts
C) Deploy T5 summarization model on Azure ML endpoints
Back:
B) Implement Azure OpenAI GPT-4 with custom summarization prompts

GPT-4 provides: flexible abstractive summarization with custom structure (issue, sentiment, actions, urgency), handles long contexts (32k tokens = ~24k words), and generates consistent format via prompt engineering. Azure AI Language summarization (A) provides extractive summaries (sentence extraction) lacking custom structure for complex requirements. T5 ML deployment (C) requires model management and lacks GPT-4's reasoning capabilities.

Reference: Summarization with Azure OpenAI
Extra: Summarization approaches: (1) Extractive: Select key sentences from original text (preserves exact wording, Azure AI Language), (2) Abstractive: Generate new summary text (paraphrases, adds context, GPT-4). For structured summaries with specific fields: Use GPT-4 with system prompt defining output schema + few-shot examples. For simple sentence extraction: Use Azure AI Language extractive summarization. Token limits: GPT-4-32k handles 24k words; for longer documents, implement recursive summarization (chunk → summarize → combine).

Front:
A healthcare imaging application must detect 12 types of medical conditions in X-ray images with 98% sensitivity (recall). The model must explain predictions with heat maps highlighting regions of concern, handle class imbalance (some conditions are rare), and comply with FDA requirements for AI medical devices requiring model auditability.

A) Train Azure AI Custom Vision object detection model with data augmentation
B) Implement Azure Machine Learning with GRAD-CAM explainability and model registry
C) Use Azure AI Vision image analysis with custom tags
Back:
B) Implement Azure Machine Learning with GRAD-CAM explainability and model registry

Azure ML provides: complete MLOps for training custom models with class imbalance handling (weighted loss, oversampling), GRAD-CAM for visual explainability (heat maps), model registry with versioning/lineage for FDA compliance, and responsible AI dashboard. Custom Vision (A) is for simpler scenarios without advanced explainability. Vision image analysis (C) uses prebuilt models without healthcare-specific training.

Reference: Responsible AI in Azure ML
Extra: Model explainability for images: (1) GRAD-CAM: Generates heat map showing important regions, (2) LIME: Perturbs image regions to measure impact on predictions, (3) Integrated Gradients: Attributes prediction to input pixels. For healthcare AI: FDA requires model cards documenting: intended use, training data, performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity), limitations, and explainability. Azure ML Responsible AI dashboard provides: error analysis, fairness metrics, model explainability, counterfactual analysis. Class imbalance solutions: focal loss, oversampling minority classes, multi-stage training.

Front:

Front:

Front:

B) Implement Azure OpenAI Assistants API with function calling

Authentication: OAuth 2.0 with Azure AD for SSO. Dialogs: Component-based conversation flows (waterfall, prompt, adaptive). Front:
A legal AI application must extract structured entities from contracts including: party names, dates, monetary amounts, obligations, termination clauses, and jurisdiction. For long documents: Use chunking strategy (process sections, deduplicate entities). Entity relationships: Custom NER extracts entities; use GPT-4 for relationship extraction ("Company A [party] must pay $10M [amount] by Dec 2024 [date]"). Front:

Liveness detection: User performs random gestures (blink, turn head) captured in video to prove physical presence. Person management: Create PersonGroup → Add persons → Add face samples per person (3-5 angles) → Train model → Identify. Privacy: Limited Access policy requires approval, face data encrypted at rest, Azure Stack for on-premises control.

Front:

The solution needs to match products by visual similarity (color, shape, pattern) rather than text tags, return top 20 similar products in <500ms, and support continuous inventory updates without retraining entire model.

B) Train Azure AI Custom Vision with similarity ranking

Back:

Custom Vision (B) is for classification/detection, not similarity ranking. Cognitive Search with tags (C) relies on text metadata, not visual similarity.

Reference: Image retrieval overview
Extra: Visual search architecture: (1) Offline: Generate embeddings for all inventory images using Vision Image Retrieval API (4096-dim vectors), index in Azure AI Search vector field, (2) Online: User uploads image → Generate query embedding → Vector search returns top K similar products by cosine similarity. Vector search algorithms: HNSW (fast, approximate) or Exhaustive KNN (exact, slower). Incremental updates: Add new products by generating embeddings and indexing without retraining. Use cases: Fashion (find similar outfits), furniture (visual style matching), parts identification (find replacement components).

Extra: Custom Vision domain options: (1) General: Versatile object detection, (2) Logo: Optimized for brand/logo detection in diverse conditions, (3) Products: Items on shelves, (4) Compact domains: Mobile/IoT deployment. Data augmentation: Custom Vision automatically applies: rotation, scaling, flipping, brightness/contrast variation to training data (improves robustness). For 90% recall: Collect negative examples (scenes without logos), balance training data across 50 brands, use advanced training (longer training time, better accuracy). Front:

Extra: Content Safety categories: (1) Violence: Graphic violence, weapons, (2) Hate: Discrimination based on identity, (3) Sexual: Sexually explicit content, (4) Self-harm: Suicide, self-injury content. Severity: 0-1 (safe) to 6-7 (high severity). Custom categories: Train on domain-specific samples (e.g., gambling content, medical misinformation). Prompt shields: Detect jailbreak attempts, user/document prompt injection attacks. Workflow: Content → Content Safety API → If severity > threshold, block publication → If borderline (medium severity), queue for human review → Log decisions for audit. Compliance: GDPR, regional content laws via configurable thresholds.

Front:

Back:
A) Implement Azure API Management with rate limiting policies, quota management, and Azure Monitor alerts

Built-in OpenAI limits (B) are service-level, not user-level. Custom middleware (C) requires infrastructure management and lacks enterprise policy features.

Reference: APIM policies
Extra: APIM policies for Azure OpenAI: (1) Rate limit: `<rate-limit calls="100" renewal-period="60" />` (100 calls/min), (2) Quota: `<quota calls="50000" bandwidth="40960" renewal-period="2629800" />` (monthly limits), (3) Usage tracking: Extract token counts from OpenAI response headers, log to Application Insights with user dimensions. Cost control: Configure Azure Cost Management budget → Alert at 80% threshold → APIM policy blocks requests at 100% quota. Fairness: Assign subscription keys per user/team with individual quotas. Circuit breaker: When OpenAI returns 429 (rate limit), return cached responses or queue requests.

Front:

B) Use Azure Logic Apps to orchestrate model calls and tool integrations

Back:

Logic Apps (B) lack automatic function selection by LLM. Durable Functions (C) are for workflow orchestration, not LLM-driven tool selection.

Reference: Function calling overview
Extra: Function calling workflow: (1) Define functions: Provide JSON schema with name, description, parameters (types, required fields), (2) Initial call: Send user message + function definitions to model, (3) Model response: Returns function name + arguments if tools needed, (4) Execute function: Validate parameters → Call backend API with auth → Get results, (5) Send results to model: Model generates final response incorporating function results. Security: Validate function parameters (prevent injection attacks), authenticate API calls with managed identity, authorize user access to requested data. Error handling: If function fails, return error to model → model reformulates request or informs user. Use Application Insights to log: function calls, parameters, execution time, errors.

C) Deploy Azure Firewall with custom threat intelligence and DDoS protection
Back:
A) Implement Azure Content Safety prompt shields, custom blocklists, and diagnostic logging to Log Analytics

Azure Content Safety provides: prompt shields (detect direct and indirect prompt injection/jailbreak), custom blocklists (block competitor names, sensitive terms), severity scoring, and integrates with Azure Monitor for audit logging. Application Gateway WAF (B) protects infrastructure but doesn't understand LLM-specific attacks. Azure Firewall (C) is network-level protection, not content-aware AI security.

Reference: Prompt shields documentation
Extra: Azure Content Safety for LLMs: (1) Prompt shields: Detect user input attacks (jailbreaks, role-playing to bypass rules) and document attacks (malicious instructions embedded in retrieved context), (2) Content filters: Analyze model outputs for harmful content (violence, hate, sexual, self-harm), (3) Blocklists: Custom term lists (up to 10,000 terms per list), supports wildcards. Security architecture: User input → Prompt shield analysis → Blocklist check → Send to LLM → Output content filter → Return to user. All blocks logged with: timestamp, user ID, attack type, severity, blocked content (for audit). Compliance: Logs meet regulatory requirements (SOC 2, GDPR) for AI safety documentation.

Front:
An Azure OpenAI fine-tuning project must customize GPT-4 for medical Q&A where the model needs to: adopt specific terminology from medical institution, improve accuracy on rare diseases, maintain consistency with clinical guidelines, and reduce hallucinations on medical facts. The solution requires baseline performance measurement, training data preparation, and validation against held-out medical exam questions.

B) Implement prompt engineering with few-shot examples and RAG pattern
C) Deploy custom pre-trained medical BERT model on Azure ML
Back:
A) Prepare JSONL training data with prompt-completion pairs, use Azure OpenAI fine-tuning API, validate with evaluation dataset

Azure OpenAI fine-tuning: Customizes model behavior on domain-specific data (100-500+ examples), improves factual accuracy and terminology consistency, reduces hallucinations through exposure to correct responses. Validation measures: accuracy on held-out exam questions vs. baseline. Prompt engineering (B) helps but doesn't fundamentally adapt model weights. Custom BERT (C) requires significant ML infrastructure and lacks GPT-4's reasoning capabilities.

Reference: Fine-tuning Azure OpenAI models
Extra: Fine-tuning process: (1) Data preparation: Create JSONL with {"prompt": "question", "completion": "answer"} pairs, minimum 100 examples (500+ for production), (2) Baseline: Evaluate base model performance on validation set, (3) Training: Upload data → Create fine-tuning job → Monitor training loss, (4) Validation: Test fine-tuned model on held-out questions → Compare accuracy to baseline, (5) Deployment: Deploy as custom model in Azure OpenAI resource. Medical use case: Collect Q&A pairs from medical guidelines, clinical protocols, rare disease documentation. Safety: Fine-tuning doesn't replace content safety - still apply Content Safety filters on outputs. Cost: Training charges per 1000 tokens, inference same as base model.

Back:

Separate Speech+Vision (B) requires custom orchestration and correlation. Media Services (C) provide streaming but lack AI analysis capabilities.

Reference: Video Indexer overview
Extra: Video Indexer insights for education: (1) Transcription: Speaker-separated transcripts with custom vocabulary (technical terms), (2) Topics/Keywords: Extracts main subjects discussed (chapter markers), (3) OCR: Reads text from slides/diagrams, (4) Visual content: Detects scene changes, identifies visual content types, (5) Sentiment: Tracks speaker emotion (enthusiasm, confusion). Custom language model: Train on course materials to improve domain term accuracy. Quiz generation pattern: Extract keywords/topics from insights → Generate questions using Azure OpenAI GPT-4 with transcript chunks as context → Validate answer accuracy against source. Workflow: Upload video → Video Indexer processing → Export insights JSON → Generate chapter markers + quiz questions → Store in learning platform.

Front:
An AI model monitoring solution must track model performance degradation over time, detect data drift in production inputs compared to training data, identify fairness issues across demographic groups, measure prediction latency, and trigger automated retraining when accuracy drops below 85%. The solution needs integration with Azure ML and Azure OpenAI models.

A) Implement Azure Machine Learning model monitoring with data drift detection, performance tracking, and fairness metrics
B) Use Azure Monitor with custom metrics and alert rules
C) Deploy Application Insights with custom telemetry tracking
Back:
A) Implement Azure Machine Learning model monitoring with data drift detection, performance tracking, and fairness metrics

Azure ML model monitoring provides: automatic data drift detection (compares production data distribution to baseline), performance metrics tracking (accuracy, precision, recall over time), fairness assessment across features, and triggers for retraining pipelines. Azure Monitor (B) is generic observability without ML-specific drift detection. Application Insights (C) tracks telemetry but lacks automated drift detection and fairness analysis.

Reference: Model monitoring in Azure ML
Extra: Model monitoring components: (1) Data drift: Detects statistical distribution changes in features (covariate shift) using Wasserstein distance, population stability index (PSI), (2) Prediction drift: Monitors output distribution changes, (3) Performance: Tracks accuracy/F1/AUC if ground truth labels available, (4) Fairness: Compares metrics across demographic groups (disparity in false positive rates). Monitoring workflow: Production predictions logged → Scheduled monitoring job compares against baseline → Drift score calculated → If threshold exceeded, trigger alert + retraining pipeline. Azure OpenAI monitoring: Track token usage, latency, content filter blocks, error rates using diagnostic logs to Log Analytics. Set up alerts: Accuracy < threshold, drift score > 0.3, latency > 2s.

Front:
An Azure Form Recognizer solution must process invoices from 100 different vendors with varying layouts, extracting standard fields (invoice number, date, total, vendor, line items) while handling poor scan quality and rotated images. The solution needs to minimize training effort and support 50,000 invoices monthly.

A) Use Form Recognizer prebuilt invoice model with confidence score validation
B) Train 100 separate custom template models (one per vendor)
C) Use Form Recognizer custom neural model with 500 labeled samples
Back:
A) Use Form Recognizer prebuilt invoice model with confidence score validation

The prebuilt invoice model is trained on millions of global invoices with varying layouts and automatically extracts 30+ standard fields including vendor, date, total, line items. It handles rotation and poor quality automatically. Training 100 custom models (B) is unnecessary and creates management overhead. Custom neural (C) is only needed when prebuilt model's field coverage is insufficient.

Reference: Prebuilt invoice model
Extra: Prebuilt invoice model supports: automatic layout variance handling, 30+ fields (VendorName, InvoiceTotal, InvoiceDate, DueDate, CustomerName, LineItems with description/quantity/unitPrice), confidence scores for validation. Use prebuilt when: extracting standard invoice fields across varying layouts. Use custom template when: fixed vendor-specific layout with non-standard fields. Use custom neural when: semantic field extraction needed ("installation fee" means "ServiceCharge"). Rotation and quality: Form Recognizer automatically corrects rotation and enhances poor scans.

Front:
A compliance application must extract and redact 25 types of PII from customer emails stored in Azure Blob Storage before archiving to Data Lake. The solution needs to identify: SSNs, credit cards, passport numbers, medical record numbers, IP addresses, and domain-specific employee IDs (format: EMP-####-YYYY). Processing 200,000 emails daily with detailed audit trail of all redactions.

A) Azure AI Language PII detection combined with custom regex patterns and Azure Event Grid for audit logging
B) Azure Cognitive Search with built-in PII detection and custom skills for employee IDs
C) Custom NER model in Azure Machine Learning with regex post-processing
Back:
A) Azure AI Language PII detection combined with custom regex patterns and Azure Event Grid for audit logging

Azure AI Language PII detection identifies 30+ standard PII types including SSN, credit cards, passport, medical records, IPs. For custom patterns (employee IDs), add regex validation in processing pipeline. Event Grid publishes redaction events for audit trail. Cognitive Search (B) is for indexing, not batch PII redaction at 200k scale. Custom NER (C) requires training data and ML infrastructure for standard PII types already handled by PII API.

Reference: PII detection
Extra: PII categories: Person, Organization, Address, Email, URL, IP, Phone, SSN, Credit card, Passport, Driver license, Medical record number, Bank account, Date of birth. Redaction strategies: (1) Replace with category [SSN], (2) Replace with asterisks (**-**-****), (3) Hash for referential integrity. Audit trail: Log {document_id, pii_type, position, redaction_timestamp, user_context}. Custom patterns: Use Named Entity Recognition regex for company-specific IDs, combine with PII API results. Compliance: GDPR Right to Erasure, CCPA, HIPAA require PII identification and protective measures.

Front:

B) Use Azure Vision Custom Vision object detection with real-time streaming SDK

Back:

Custom Vision (B) detects objects but lacks audio analysis and timeline correlation. Media Services (C) provides streaming infrastructure without AI analysis.

Reference: Custom brands model
Extra: Video Indexer brands detection: Visual (logo/product detection in frames) + Audio (brand name mentions in transcription). Custom brands model: Upload product images → Train visual detection; Add brand names/aliases → Detect in audio. Timeline insights: Correlate visual appearance + audio mentions with timestamps. Live ingestion: Ingest RTMP stream → Real-time processing → Streaming insights API. Use cases: Product placement ROI (visibility duration × reach), sponsorship validation (contracted vs actual airtime), competitive monitoring (competitor product appearances). Output: JSON insights with {brand, appearance_type (visual/audio/both), duration, confidence}.

Front:
An enterprise knowledge base must provide natural language Q&A over 50,000 technical documents with: multi-turn conversation support, confidence scoring above 80%, source attribution with specific document sections, active learning from user feedback on answer quality, and handling unanswerable questions gracefully.

A) Create Azure AI Language custom question answering project with multi-turn, metadata filtering, active learning
B) Implement Azure OpenAI with RAG using Azure AI Search for retrieval
C) Deploy Azure Bot Framework with LUIS intent detection and Document Intelligence
Back:

Custom Question Answering provides: structured Q&A pairs with variants, multi-turn conversations (follow-up prompts), confidence scores, source attribution, active learning (suggests improvements from user feedback), graceful "I don't know" responses. RAG with OpenAI (B) generates answers but lacks built-in active learning and structured confidence thresholds. Bot Framework + LUIS (C) handles conversations but requires custom Q&A implementation.

Reference: Custom question answering
Extra: Custom QA features: (1) Multi-turn: Define follow-up prompts ("Which Azure service?" → "App Service" → "Show pricing"), (2) Synonyms: Map "VM" to "virtual machine", (3) Metadata filtering: Tag QA pairs by category/version, (4) Active learning: System suggests new Q&A pairs from user queries without good matches, (5) Chitchat: Pre-built personality responses for small talk. Confidence scoring: Threshold at 80% → High confidence answers returned; 50-80% → Show multiple suggestions; <50% → "I don't know" + escalate. Source attribution: Returns source document URL + character offset. Training: Import FAQ docs, edit Q&A pairs, publish knowledge base.

Front:
A speech-enabled application must transcribe customer service calls in real-time with: custom vocabulary for 500 product names, speaker separation (agent vs customer), sentiment analysis per utterance, profanity masking for compliance, and keyword spotting for escalation triggers ("cancel subscription", "complaint"). Average call duration 10 minutes, 1000 concurrent calls.

A) Azure Speech Service with custom speech model, speaker diarization, Speech SDK sentiment intent recognition
B) Azure Video Indexer audio analysis with custom models
C) Azure AI Language speech-to-text with post-processing Azure Functions
Back:

Azure Speech provides: custom speech models (add product vocabulary), real-time speaker diarization (agent vs customer labels), intent recognition with sentiment for utterances, profanity filter, and keyword spotting. Video Indexer (B) is for pre-recorded video/audio, not real-time streaming. AI Language STT (C) is not a speech service; Speech Service is the correct Azure offering.

Reference: Intent recognition
Extra: Speech Service real-time features: (1) Custom speech: Add domain vocabulary (product names, jargon), (2) Speaker diarization: Returns speaker labels per segment ("Speaker 1: Hello", "Speaker 2: How can I help?"), (3) Intent recognition: Combines STT with Language Understanding (detect intents like "CancelSubscription", extract entities), (4) Sentiment: Per-utterance sentiment scores, (5) Profanity filter: Mask/remove/raw modes, (6) Keyword spotting: Client-side wake word detection. Deployment: WebSocket streaming via Speech SDK, results in real-time (<1s latency). Use Application Insights to aggregate: call sentiment trends, escalation keyword frequency, speaker talk time ratio (agent vs customer).

Front:
A global content platform must translate 1 million user-generated articles monthly from 20 source languages into English while: preserving technical terminology (programming keywords, API names), maintaining HTML/Markdown formatting, handling mixed-language content (code snippets in English within Spanish text), and achieving cost-effective batch processing with 24-hour SLA.

A) Azure Translator document translation API with custom dictionaries and batch operations
B) Azure OpenAI GPT-4 with translation prompts and parallel processing
C) Azure AI Language translation with sequential API calls per article
Back:
A) Azure Translator document translation API with custom dictionaries and batch operations

Translator document translation: Preserves HTML/Markdown formatting, custom dictionaries (preserve technical terms), batch operations (submit 1000s of documents in single request), cost-effective for high volume, handles mixed content. GPT-4 (B) is 10-50x more expensive per word and slower for batch translation. AI Language (C) requires sequential per-article calls (less efficient than batch operations).

Reference: Document translation
Extra: Document translation vs text translation: Text API translates strings (requires format preservation logic), Document API translates files (preserves formatting automatically). Supported formats: PDF, HTML, DOCX, XLSX, Markdown, plain text. Custom dictionary: Define term mappings {"API": "API", "async/await": "async/await"} to prevent translation. Batch workflow: Upload source docs to Blob Storage → Submit translation job with target languages + glossary → Monitor status → Download translated docs from output container. Cost: Charged per character translated. Mixed content: Detected language spans handled separately (code blocks remain unchanged if marked). Glossary size limit: 10MB per dictionary.

Front:
An AI search solution must enable users to find documents by asking natural language questions ("What are the backup retention policies?") rather than keywords. The solution requires: semantic understanding of query intent, answer extraction from documents, highlighted passages explaining relevance, integration with existing Azure Cognitive Search index (5M documents), and <1 second query latency.

A) Enable semantic search and answers features in Azure AI Search
B) Implement Azure OpenAI with RAG pattern querying the search index
C) Deploy Azure AI Language custom question answering trained on all documents
Back:
A) Enable semantic search and answers features in Azure AI Search

Azure AI Search semantic search uses deep learning models to understand query intent beyond keywords, ranks results by semantic relevance, extracts answers from documents, and provides captions (highlighted passages). Sub-second latency at scale. RAG with OpenAI (B) adds 2-5s latency for generation. Custom QA (C) requires extracting Q&A pairs (not scalable for 5M general documents).

Reference: Semantic search
Extra: Semantic search architecture: User query → BM25 retrieval (top 1000) → Semantic L2 reranking (top 50 using ML models) → Results with captions + answers. Semantic ranking: Uses transformer models trained on web data to understand query meaning and match semantically similar content. Captions: Extracts relevant passage (200 chars) from document explaining why it matched. Answers: Extracts direct answer to question-style queries ("What is X?" → "X is..."). Enable steps: Upgrade to Standard tier → Enable semantic configuration → Add "semantic" query type. Latency: Adds 100-300ms vs keyword search. Cost: Charged per 1000 semantic queries. Best for: Question answering, conceptual search ("documents about cost reduction").

Front:
A document enrichment pipeline must extract custom entities from legal contracts (case numbers, statute references, contract parties, jurisdiction) using 200 labeled training samples, enrich documents in Azure Cognitive Search during indexing, maintain entity metadata for faceted search, and support continuous model improvement as new contract types are encountered.

A) Train Azure AI Language custom NER model, deploy as custom skill in Azure Cognitive Search skillset
B) Use Azure Form Recognizer composed models for each contract type
C) Implement Azure Functions with regex patterns as custom search skill
Back:
A) Train Azure AI Language custom NER model, deploy as custom skill in Azure Cognitive Search skillset

Custom NER extracts domain-specific entities from unstructured text with trained model (learns from labeled examples), deploys as a web API that can be called from Cognitive Search custom web API skill during indexing. Form Recognizer (B) is for structured form extraction, not entity extraction from unstructured contract clauses. Regex (C) is brittle and doesn't learn entity patterns from context.

Reference: Custom skills
Extra: Custom skill integration: (1) Train custom NER model in Language Studio with entity types (CaseNumber, Statute, Party, Jurisdiction), (2) Deploy model to endpoint, (3) Create custom web API skill in search skillset calling NER endpoint, (4) Map extracted entities to index fields, (5) Enable facets on entity fields for filtering. Skillset execution: Document cracking → Built-in skills (OCR, language detection) → Custom NER skill (entity extraction) → Output field mapping → Index document. Continuous improvement: New contract types → Label additional examples → Retrain model → Redeploy → Incremental reindex. Faceted search: Users filter by {Jurisdiction: California, Party: Acme Corp}.

Front:
An Azure AI Search index must support both keyword search and vector similarity search for 2 million product descriptions, enabling users to search by text query or upload images to find visually similar products. The solution requires: hybrid ranking combining text relevance and visual similarity, filtering by product attributes (category, price, brand), and <200ms query latency at 100 QPS.

A) Configure Azure AI Search with vector fields for image embeddings, hybrid queries with RRF ranking, and filterable fields
B) Use separate indexes for text (Azure AI Search) and vectors (Azure Cosmos DB), merge results in application
C) Deploy Azure Databricks with FAISS for vector search and Elasticsearch for text search
Back:
A) Configure Azure AI Search with vector fields for image embeddings, hybrid queries with RRF ranking, and filterable fields

Azure AI Search natively supports: vector fields (store embeddings), hybrid queries (combine vector similarity + full-text + filters in single query), reciprocal rank fusion (RRF) for hybrid result ranking, sub-200ms latency. Separate systems (B) require custom result merging and ranking. Databricks + external search (C) adds infrastructure complexity and lacks unified query API.

Reference: Vector search queries
Extra: Hybrid search implementation: (1) Indexing: Text fields (description) + vector field (image_embedding from Azure Vision), (2) Query execution: Text query → BM25 scoring + Image upload → Generate embedding → Vector similarity scoring → RRF combines scores → Apply filters → Return results. RRF (Reciprocal Rank Fusion): Merges rankings from multiple search systems (text + vector) by computing 1/(rank+k) for each result in each ranking, then summing. Vector indexing algorithms: HNSW (approximate nearest neighbor, fast) or Exhaustive KNN (exact results, slower). Filtering: Applied before vector search (pre-filtering) or after (post-filtering) based on performance needs. Image embeddings: Generate using Azure Computer Vision Image Retrieval API (4096-dim vectors).

Front:
A knowledge mining solution must process 500,000 scanned PDF documents with images and tables, extracting text via OCR, identifying people/organizations/locations, translating non-English content to English, detecting key phrases, and projecting enriched data to table storage for analytics. The pipeline runs nightly with change detection to avoid reprocessing unchanged documents.

B) Implement Azure Data Factory with Cognitive Services activities and Azure Synapse staging
C) Use Azure Logic Apps with document processing connectors and Cosmos DB for outputs
Back:
A) Create Azure Cognitive Search indexer with enrichment skillset, knowledge store projections, and change detection

Cognitive Search indexers orchestrate: document cracking (read PDFs), skillset execution (OCR → NER → key phrases → translation), knowledge store projections (export enriched data to tables/objects for analytics), incremental indexing (high water mark change detection). Data Factory (B) requires custom orchestration. Logic Apps (C) don't scale to 500k documents nightly.

Reference: Knowledge store
Extra: Enrichment pipeline: PDF → Document cracking (extract text/images) → OCR skill (images to text) → Merge skill (combine) → Language detection → Entity Recognition (people, orgs, locations) → Key phrase extraction → Translation (if non-English) → Output to index + knowledge store. Knowledge store: Projects enriched data to Azure Storage (tables for analytics, objects for downstream processing). Projections types: Object (JSON blobs), Table (flattened rows), File (normalized images). Change detection: High water mark (LastModified timestamp) or integrated change tracking. Skillset caching: Reuses intermediary enrichments when documents change partially (saves time/cost). Built-in skills: 15+ cognitive skills; add custom skills for domain logic.

Front:
A generative AI application must implement retrieval-augmented generation where: user queries retrieve relevant documents from 100,000 internal Wiki pages using semantic search, top 5 documents provide context to GPT-4 for answer generation, responses cite source pages with hyperlinks, and the system tracks retrieval relevance scores to improve search quality over time.

A) Implement RAG pattern: Azure AI Search with semantic ranking for retrieval, Azure OpenAI for generation, Application Insights for tracking
B) Use Azure OpenAI Assistants API with file search capability
C) Deploy LangChain with Azure Cosmos DB vector search and Azure OpenAI
Back:
A) Implement RAG pattern: Azure AI Search with semantic ranking for retrieval, Azure OpenAI for generation, Application Insights for tracking

RAG architecture: User query → Azure AI Search semantic search retrieves top 5 relevant docs with scores → Send query + document context to GPT-4 → GPT generates answer with citations → Log retrieval scores to Application Insights. Assistants API (B) lacks control over retrieval ranking and citation format. LangChain (C) requires custom implementation vs. native Azure integration.

Reference: RAG overview
Extra: RAG implementation steps: (1) Indexing: Wiki pages → Chunk documents (500-1000 tokens) → Generate embeddings (Azure OpenAI text-embedding-3-large) → Index in Azure AI Search with vector + semantic config, (2) Retrieval: Query → Hybrid search (vector + semantic ranking) → Top K results with scores, (3) Generation: System prompt + query + retrieved context → GPT-4 → Answer, (4) Citation: Parse response to identify source references → Link to Wiki URLs. Tracking: Log {query, retrieval_scores, answer_quality_feedback} → Analyze low-scoring retrievals → Tune chunking/embeddings. Prompt engineering: "Answer based on provided context. Cite sources as [1], [2]...". Context window: GPT-4-32k supports ~24k words of context.

Front:
An Azure OpenAI application experiencing inconsistent response quality must implement evaluation metrics tracking: answer relevance to user queries, groundedness (answer supported by retrieved context), coherence (logical flow), and fluency. The solution needs automated evaluation on 500 test queries daily with alerts when quality metrics drop below thresholds (relevance <0.8, groundedness <0.9).

A) Deploy Azure Prompt Flow with evaluation flows using GPT-4 as evaluator and Azure Monitor alerts
B) Implement custom Azure Functions calling OpenAI API with regex-based quality checks
C) Use Azure Machine Learning with traditional NLP metrics (BLEU, ROUGE, F1)
Back:
A) Deploy Azure Prompt Flow with evaluation flows using GPT-4 as evaluator and Azure Monitor alerts

Azure Prompt Flow provides: evaluation flows that use GPT-4 to score responses on custom criteria (relevance, groundedness, coherence, fluency), batch evaluation on test sets, integration with Azure Monitor for alerting on metric thresholds. Custom functions (B) with regex can't evaluate semantic quality. Traditional metrics (C) measure n-gram overlap, not semantic correctness or factual grounding.

Reference: Evaluate prompt flows
Extra: Prompt Flow evaluation: (1) Define evaluation criteria with GPT-4 prompts ("Rate answer relevance 0-1"), (2) Create evaluation flow: Input (query, answer, context) → GPT-4 evaluator → Scores, (3) Run batch evaluation on test set (500 queries), (4) Aggregate metrics: avg relevance, groundedness, coherence, fluency, (5) Configure Monitor alerts (relevance <0.8 → send notification). Evaluation metrics: Relevance (answer addresses query?), Groundedness (claims supported by context?), Coherence (logical flow?), Fluency (grammatically correct?). GPT-4 as judge: Provide rubric in system prompt, use structured output for scores. Alternative: Azure ML Responsible AI dashboard for model evaluation (fairness, error analysis). Track over time: Detect quality degradation → investigate prompt changes, model updates, data drift.

Front:
An enterprise AI solution must implement content filtering for customer-facing chatbot to prevent: generation of harmful content (violence, hate), disclosure of confidential information in prompt injections, jailbreak attempts bypassing system instructions, and toxic user inputs. The system requires real-time blocking with detailed logging of violations for compliance audits, processing 50,000 conversations daily.

A) Enable Azure OpenAI content filters (input/output) and Azure Content Safety prompt shields with diagnostic logging
B) Implement Azure Application Gateway WAF with custom rules for AI-specific threats
C) Use Azure Firewall Premium with TLS inspection and threat intelligence
Back:
A) Enable Azure OpenAI content filters (input/output) and Azure Content Safety prompt shields with diagnostic logging

Azure OpenAI content filters block harmful content in both user inputs and model outputs (violence, hate, sexual, self-harm) with configurable severity thresholds. Content Safety prompt shields detect jailbreak attempts and prompt injections. Both integrate with Azure Monitor diagnostic logs for audit trails. WAF (B) protects web infrastructure, not AI content. Firewall (C) is network-level security without content-aware AI threat detection.

Reference: Content filtering
Extra: Content filtering layers: (1) Input filters: Block harmful user prompts before sending to model, (2) Output filters: Block harmful model generations before returning to user, (3) Prompt shields: Detect jailbreaks ("Ignore previous instructions"), prompt injections (malicious instructions in user content). Severity levels: 0-1 safe, 2-3 low, 4-5 medium, 6-7 high. Configuration: Set thresholds per category (block violence >=4, sexual >=2). Annotated content filters: Custom adjustments for domain-specific needs (medical content may allow anatomical terms). Logging: Diagnostic settings → Log Analytics workspace → Query blocked content types, frequency, user patterns. Compliance: Audit trail meets SOC 2, GDPR requirements. Handling blocks: Return user-friendly message ("I can't assist with that"), log details internally.

Front:
A multi-turn conversational AI agent must maintain context across sessions, remember user preferences (preferred language, notification settings), access five backend APIs (inventory, pricing, orders, shipping, CRM) via function calling, and route to human agents when confidence drops below 0.7. The agent handles 10,000 concurrent conversations with conversation state persisted across user disconnections.
