B) Implement Azure Bot Framework with LUIS and conversation state middleware
C) Deploy Azure OpenAI Assistants API with threads and file capabilities
Back:
A) Build agent in Azure AI Foundry with function tools, Cosmos DB conversation state, handoff orchestration

Azure AI Foundry (formerly Azure AI Studio) agents provide: built-in function/tool integration (declaratively define API tools), conversation memory with Cosmos DB persistence, handoff patterns (escalation to human agents), and autogen-based orchestration. Bot Framework (B) requires custom LLM integration and tool orchestration. OpenAI Assistants (C) lack enterprise features (handoff orchestration, custom state backends).

Reference: AI Foundry agents
Extra: Agent architecture: (1) Tools: Define 5 functions as tools (get_inventory, check_pricing, create_order, track_shipping, lookup_customer), (2) State: Store {conversation_id, user_id, preferences, history} in Cosmos DB, (3) Conversation flow: User message → Retrieve state → Agent plans action → Call tools if needed → Generate response → Save state, (4) Confidence monitoring: Evaluate response confidence → If <0.7 → Trigger handoff, (5) Handoff: Transfer context to human agent (Escalation topic). Tool validation: Authenticate API calls with managed identity, validate inputs against schema. Session persistence: Cosmos DB provides: low latency state access (<10ms), automatic indexing by conversation_id, global distribution for multi-region deployments. Preference retrieval: Load user preferences on session start → Configure language, tone, notification settings.

Front:
An autonomous SQL query agent must accept natural language requests ("Show total sales by region last quarter"), generate SQL queries against Azure SQL Database, execute queries safely with read-only permissions, validate results for reasonableness, and explain results in natural language. The solution must prevent SQL injection, handle ambiguous queries by asking clarifying questions, and log all generated SQL for auditing.

A) Implement Azure OpenAI function calling with SQL generation tool, parameterized queries, validation step, and Application Insights logging
B) Use Azure Synapse SQL with query generation stored procedures
C) Deploy NLP-to-SQL custom model in Azure Machine Learning
Back:
A) Implement Azure OpenAI function calling with SQL generation tool, parameterized queries, validation step, and Application Insights logging

Architecture: User NL query → GPT-4 with schema context generates SQL → Validation function checks syntax/permissions → Execute parameterized query (prevents injection) → Validate result reasonableness → GPT-4 explains results in NL. Synapse (B) is data warehouse, not NL query interface. Custom ML model (C) requires training data and lacks GPT-4's reasoning for result explanation.

Reference: Function calling
Extra: Text-to-SQL agent flow: (1) Schema injection: Provide database schema (tables, columns, relationships) in system prompt, (2) SQL generation: User query → GPT-4 generates SQL, (3) Validation: Check SQL for: SELECT only (no UPDATE/DELETE), valid table/column names, reasonable filters (date range not 100 years), (4) Parameterization: Use @param placeholders (prevent injection), (5) Execution: Run with read-only connection, timeout 30s, (6) Result explanation: SQL + results → GPT-4 → NL summary. Ambiguity handling: If query unclear ("last quarter" → need year?), agent asks clarifying question. Safety: Database user has db_datareader role only, query timeout prevents runaway queries. Logging: Application Insights records {query, generated_sql, execution_time, result_count, user_feedback}.

Front:
A document processing pipeline must analyze receipts uploaded from mobile apps, extracting: merchant name, date, total amount, payment method, line items (description, quantity, price), tax, and tip. The solution supports receipts in 50 languages and currencies, handles crumpled/faded photos, and achieves 95%+ accuracy on structured field extraction for expense tracking integration.

A) Use Azure Form Recognizer prebuilt receipt model with multi-language support
B) Train Azure AI Custom Vision classification model with OCR post-processing
C) Implement Azure Vision OCR with custom parsing logic and regex templates
Back:
A) Use Azure Form Recognizer prebuilt receipt model with multi-language support

Form Recognizer prebuilt receipt model is trained on global receipts, extracts standard fields (MerchantName, TransactionDate, Total, Tax, Tip, Items with Description/Quantity/TotalPrice), handles 50+ languages/currencies automatically, robust to photo quality issues. Custom Vision (B) is for classification, not field extraction. OCR + regex (C) is brittle across receipt layout variations and language differences.

Reference: Prebuilt receipt model
Extra: Receipt model fields: MerchantName, MerchantAddress, MerchantPhoneNumber, TransactionDate, TransactionTime, Total, Subtotal, Tax, Tip, Items (array with ItemName, Quantity, Price, TotalPrice). Multi-language: Automatically detects language and extracts text (English, Spanish, French, German, Japanese, Chinese, etc.). Currency handling: Recognizes currency symbols/codes (USD, EUR, JPY), extracts numeric amounts. Confidence scores: Each field has score 0-1 (validate total if score <0.9). Workflow: Mobile app uploads image → Form Recognizer API → JSON response with fields → Validate amounts (subtotal+tax+tip ≈ total) → Submit to expense system. Quality improvements: Preprocess images (rotation correction, contrast enhancement) before submission for optimal results. Alternatives: Use Read model for simple text extraction; use Layout model when tables needed without field semantics.

Front:
An identity verification system must compare faces from a live camera capture against a stored government ID photo to prevent fraud, detecting liveness to block photo/video replay attacks. The solution requires: face matching with confidence threshold, liveness detection within 3 seconds, privacy-preserving processing (no biometric storage after match), and support for 20,000 daily verifications with <2 second total latency.

A) Azure Face service verification API with liveness detection and face comparison
B) Azure Custom Vision with face classification model
C) Azure Form Recognizer ID document model with face extraction
Back:
A) Azure Face service verification API with liveness detection and face comparison

Azure Face provides: 1:1 face verification (compare two faces, return confidence score), liveness detection (user performs gestures to prove physical presence, blocks replay attacks), sub-2s latency, no face storage after verification (detect → compare → discard). Custom Vision (B) is for object classification, not face verification. Form Recognizer ID (C) extracts ID data but doesn't perform liveness or face matching.

Reference: Face verification
Extra: Face verification flow: (1) ID document capture: Extract face from ID using Form Recognizer ID model, (2) Liveness: User performs head turn/smile → Liveness API analyzes video → Returns liveness confidence, (3) Face comparison: Live face + ID face → Verification API → confidence score + isIdentical boolean, (4) Decision: If liveness valid AND confidence >0.7 → approve; else → reject. Liveness detection: Challenges user with random gestures (prevents static photo attacks), analyzes video frames for 3D depth and motion consistency. Privacy: Face Detection returns facial landmarks (no persistent ID), Verification uses faces immediately without storage. Confidence threshold: 0.7+ for high security scenarios (financial), 0.5+ for low-risk. Limited Access: Face API requires approval from Microsoft, used for identity verification only (not surveillance).

Front:
A manufacturing quality control system must detect product defects (scratches, dents, color inconsistencies, missing components) in images from assembly line cameras. The solution needs: defect localization with bounding boxes, classification into 12 defect types, confidence scores for automated pass/fail decisions (confidence >0.92 = fail), and handles 10,000 images daily with retraining as new defect types emerge.

A) Train Azure AI Custom Vision object detection model with 12 defect classes, export predictions, retrain iteratively
B) Use Azure Computer Vision image analysis with custom tags
C) Deploy Azure Form Recognizer custom model for defect detection
Back:
A) Train Azure AI Custom Vision object detection model with 12 defect classes, export predictions, retrain iteratively

Custom Vision object detection: Train on labeled images with defect bounding boxes, detects multiple defects per image with classifications and confidence scores, supports iterative retraining as new defect types added. Computer Vision image analysis (B) detects common objects (not domain-specific defects). Form Recognizer (C) is for document/form extraction, not image defect detection.

Reference: Custom Vision object detection
Extra: Defect detection pipeline: (1) Data collection: Label 50-100 images per defect class with bounding boxes in Custom Vision portal, (2) Training: Automatic model optimization with advanced training option (better accuracy), (3) Evaluation: Review precision/recall per class, confusion matrix, (4) Deployment: Publish as prediction API, (5) Inference: Assembly line image → Prediction API → Defect bounding boxes + class + confidence, (6) Decision: If any defect confidence >0.92 → Reject product. Retraining: New defect types → Label examples → Retrain model → A/B test new model version → Publish if improved. Active learning: Flag low-confidence predictions (0.5-0.7) for manual review and retraining. Export: Deploy to edge (ONNX/TensorFlow Lite) for offline factory floor inference with IoT Edge. Monitoring: Track defect rates by type → identify recurring issues → improve manufacturing process.

Front:
A video content platform must automatically generate metadata for uploaded videos including: scene breaks, visual content tags (objects, activities, settings), sentiment of scenes, embedded text (signs, captions), face recognition for recurring persons, and spoken content transcription with timestamps. The solution processes 1000 hours of video weekly with searchable metadata output.

A) Azure Video Indexer with custom models for faces and brands, REST API for metadata export
B) Azure Media Services with Azure Vision for frame analysis and Azure Speech for audio
C) Azure Cognitive Search indexer with video processing skillsets
Back:
A) Azure Video Indexer with custom models for faces and brands, REST API for metadata export

Video Indexer provides: comprehensive video analysis (scenes, visual tags, OCR, faces, transcription, sentiment, topics) in single API, custom face models for recurring person identification, exports JSON metadata with timestamps for all insights. Media Services (B) provides streaming infrastructure without integrated AI analysis (requires building separate analysis). Search indexer (C) doesn't process video directly.

Reference: Video Indexer
Extra: Video Indexer insights: (1) Visual: Scene/shot detection, keyframes, visual content tags ("outdoor", "car", "beach"), OCR (text in frames), faces (detection + recognition), brands/logos, (2) Audio: Transcription (speaker diarization, multi-language), audio effects (applause, laughter), sentiment per segment, (3) Content: Topics/keywords extraction, content moderation flags. Custom models: Face model (upload photos of recurring people → recognize in videos), Brand model (company-specific brands). Workflow: Upload video → Processing (async, ~video duration) → GET insights → Parse JSON → Index metadata in Azure Cognitive Search. Timeline: All insights have timestamps {start, end} for seeking to exact moments. Use cases: Media asset management (searchable archive), content moderation (detect violating scenes), audience analytics (sentiment trends), accessibility (transcriptions/captions).

Front:
A chat application must detect toxic language in user messages before publishing, identifying: profanity, personal attacks, sexual harassment, threats, hate speech based on protected characteristics. The solution needs severity scoring (low/medium/high), custom blocklists for platform-specific terms, explanation of why content was blocked for user feedback, and handling 100,000 messages daily with <200ms moderation latency.

A) Azure Content Safety text moderation with severity levels, custom categories, and reason codes
B) Azure AI Language sentiment analysis with negative sentiment filtering
C) Azure Text Analytics for profanity detection with custom regex filters
Back:
A) Azure Content Safety text moderation with severity levels, custom categories, and reason codes

Azure Content Safety provides: text moderation across harm categories (hate, sexual, violence, self-harm), severity scores 0-7, custom categories for platform-specific content (e.g. spam, bullying), reason codes explaining blocks. Sentiment analysis (B) measures positive/negative/neutral, not toxicity types. Text Analytics profanity (C) only detects profanity, not harassment/threats/hate speech.

Reference: Content Safety harm categories
Extra: Content moderation workflow: User submits message → Content Safety API → Returns {category: Violence, severity: 6, reason: "Threat detected"} → If severity >=4 → Block message and respond "Your message contains threatening language" → Log incident. Harm categories: (1) Hate: Discrimination based on identity (race, religion, gender, sexual orientation), (2) Sexual: Sexually explicit content, harassment, (3) Violence: Graphic violence, threats, (4) Self-harm: Suicide, self-injury content. Severity: 0-1 safe, 2-3 low (mild profanity), 4-5 medium (insults), 6-7 high (severe threats, hate speech). Custom categories: Train on platform-specific examples (e.g., "doxxing", "brigading"). Blocklists: Supplement with term lists (slurs, banned phrases). User feedback: Provide reason ("Message contains hate speech") with appeal option. Latency: <100ms per message, scales to 100k+ QPS.

Front:
An enterprise prompt management system for Azure OpenAI applications must support: version control of prompts, A/B testing different prompt variations, parameterization for dynamic content (user name, product details), role-based access control for prompt editing, audit logging of prompt changes, and hot-reload without application redeployment.

A) Azure App Configuration with feature flags, versioning, managed identity access control, and push refresh
B) Store prompts in Azure Blob Storage with versioning enabled
C) Use Azure DevOps Repos with CI/CD pipeline for prompt deployment
Back:
A) Azure App Configuration with feature flags, versioning, managed identity access control, and push refresh

App Configuration provides: key-value storage with labels (version control), feature flags (A/B testing prompt variants), push refresh (hot-reload via Event Grid), managed identity RBAC (control who edits prompts), audit via Activity Log. Blob Storage (B) requires custom versioning/access logic and no hot-reload. DevOps Repos (C) require redeployment for prompt changes (no hot-reload).

Reference: Azure App Configuration
Extra: Prompt management architecture: (1) Storage: Prompts as key-value pairs with labels (v1, v2, production, staging), (2) Parameterization: Template with {user_name}, {product_name} placeholders → replaced at runtime, (3) A/B testing: Feature flags route 50% to prompt_v1, 50% to prompt_v2 → track performance metrics, (4) Access control: Assign "App Configuration Data Owner" role to prompt engineers (edit), "Data Reader" to applications (read-only), (5) Hot-reload: App Configuration sends Event Grid notification on change → application refreshes prompts without restart. Audit trail: Azure Activity Log records prompt modifications {timestamp, user, old_value, new_value}. Versioning strategy: Label by date (2024-03-24), environment (prod, staging), or model (gpt4, gpt35). Best practices: Store system prompts, few-shot examples, constraints ("max 500 words"), temperature settings. Avoid: Storing API keys (use Key Vault instead).

Front:
A research application using Azure OpenAI must generate citations for factual claims in responses by: tracking which retrieved documents support each statement, providing inline citations with source URLs, enabling click-through to exact document sections, distinguishing between high-confidence facts (multiple sources) and low-confidence claims (single source), and validating citations accuracy with 90%+ precision.

A) Implement RAG with Azure AI Search, configure Azure OpenAI on your data citation feature, validate with groundedness evaluation
B) Use Azure OpenAI with custom citation parsing from model responses
C) Deploy LangChain document retrievers with citation metadata
Back:
A) Implement RAG with Azure AI Search, configure Azure OpenAI on your data citation feature, validate with groundedness evaluation

Azure OpenAI on your data: Automatically generates citations by tracking which retrieved chunks support each claim, returns citation metadata (source document, chunk ID, URL), formats inline citations. Groundedness evaluation scores whether claims are supported by sources. Custom parsing (B) is unreliable as model may hallucinate citations. LangChain (C) requires custom citation tracking logic.

Reference: Azure OpenAI on your data
Extra: Citation implementation: (1) Indexing: Documents chunked → metadata includes {source_url, section, chunk_id} → indexed in Azure AI Search, (2) Retrieval: Query → search returns top K chunks with metadata, (3) Generation: System prompt instructs model to cite sources using [doc1], [doc2] format → Sends chunks + metadata to GPT-4, (4) Response parsing: Extract citation markers → map to source URLs → return citations array. Inline citations: "Azure AI provides 100+ services [1]. It includes vision, language, and speech capabilities[1][2]." with citations = [{id: 1, url: "https://...", title: "..."}]. Confidence signals: Multiple citations for same claim = high confidence, single citation = verify. Groundedness evaluation: GPT-4 evaluator prompt: "Is this claim supported by the provided context?" → score 0-1 → validate >90% claims have score >0.9. Validation: Sample responses → human review citation accuracy → retrain embeddings or adjust chunking if precision <90%.

Front:
A product design team needs an AI tool to generate multiple creative product descriptions in marketing, technical, and casual tones for 5,000 SKUs. The solution must: generate 3 variations per SKU, maintain factual accuracy about product specs, control output length (100-150 words), prevent repetitive phrasing across generations, and allow human review before publishing.

A) Azure OpenAI GPT-4 with temperature=0.8, max_tokens=200, presence_penalty=0.6, multiple completions, human-in-loop approval flow
B) Azure Text Analytics key phrase extraction with template filling
C) Azure Translator with style variation options
Back:
A) Azure OpenAI GPT-4 with temperature=0.8, max_tokens=200, presence_penalty=0.6, multiple completions, human-in-loop approval flow

GPT-4 with creativity settings: temperature=0.8 generates varied creative outputs, presence_penalty=0.6 discourages repetition, n=3 parameter generates 3 variations in single API call, max_tokens controls length. Key phrase extraction (B) doesn't generate creative text. Translator (C) translates, doesn't generate varied marketing copy.

Reference: Completion parameters
Extra: Creative generation parameters: (1) Temperature: 0.0 = deterministic, 1.0 = creative; use 0.7-0.9 for marketing copy, (2) Top_p: Nucleus sampling (0.95 for diversity), (3) Presence_penalty: 0-2, penalizes repeated tokens (0.6 reduces repetition), (4) Frequency_penalty: 0-2, penalizes common tokens (0.3 encourages unique phrasing), (5) N: Generate multiple completions per request. Prompt engineering: "Generate {tone} product description for {product_name}. Specs: {specs}. Length: 100-150 words. Tone: {marketing|technical|casual}." Workflow: For each SKU → Generate 3 variations → Store in database → Human reviews via approval UI → Publish approved version. Factual accuracy: Include product specs in prompt, use temperature 0.0 for specs extraction, 0.8 for creative prose. Token budget: 150 words ≈ 200 tokens (set max_tokens=200, stop sequence "\n\n" for clean endings). Batch processing: Use batch API for 5,000 SKUs to reduce cost 50%.

Front:
An Azure Cognitive Search solution with 10 million documents experiences slow indexing (6 hours for full rebuild) and query latency spikes during peak hours. The solution requires: indexing time reduced to <2 hours, consistent sub-second query latency at 200 QPS, and cost optimization. Documents are 50KB average, index includes 20 fields with 5 searchable text fields and vector embeddings.

A) Increase search service replicas for query performance, enable parallel indexing, optimize index schema by reducing analyzers
B) Upgrade to higher search tier with more partitions
C) Implement caching layer with Azure Redis Cache
Back:
A) Increase search service replicas for query performance, enable parallel indexing, optimize index schema by reducing analyzers

Performance optimization: Replicas scale query throughput (add replicas for 200 QPS), partitions scale storage/indexing throughput. Parallel indexing (batch size, degree of parallelism) speeds full rebuild. Reduce analyzers on non-essential fields speeds indexing. Upgrading tier (B) increases cost unnecessarily if optimization resolves issues. Redis caching (C) adds complexity and doesn't address core search performance.

Reference: Search performance optimization
Extra: Search performance tuning: (1) Replicas: Scale query load (each replica handles reads), recommended 3+ for HA, 10+ for high QPS, (2) Partitions: Scale index size and indexing throughput (each partition stores data subset), (3) Indexing: Batch documents (up to 1000 per request), parallel indexing (concurrent batches), disable scoring/suggestions during initial load, (4) Schema optimization: Reduce analyzers (language analyzers are slow), make fields "retrievable" only if needed in results, use "filterable" instead of "searchable" for exact-match fields, (5) Vector optimization: Use smaller dimensions (1536 vs 3072), HNSW algorithm (faster than exhaustive). Cost optimization: Standard S1 (15GB, $250/mo) → S2 (60GB, $1000/mo); add replicas before upgrading tier. Query latency: <100ms for simple queries, <1s for complex (facets, semantic ranking, vector search). Monitoring: Track query QPS, indexing doc/sec, storage utilization.

Front:
A compliance-driven AI application must ensure: all Azure OpenAI API calls are encrypted in transit and at rest, API keys rotated monthly, access restricted to specific virtual networks, requests/responses logged for audit (excluding PII), data residency within EU region, and no data used for model improvement by OpenAI.

A) Deploy Azure OpenAI in EU region with private endpoints, managed identity authentication, diagnostic logging to Log Analytics with data export controls, data processing opt-out
B) Use Azure OpenAI with shared keys stored in Azure Key Vault
C) Deploy Azure Kubernetes Service with self-hosted OpenAI models
Back:
A) Deploy Azure OpenAI in EU region with private endpoints, managed identity authentication, diagnostic logging to Log Analytics with data export controls, data processing opt-out

Security & compliance: Azure OpenAI in EU ensures data residency, private endpoints restrict network access, managed identities eliminate key management, diagnostic logs capture requests (opt-out PII fields), data processing opt-out prevents using data for model training. Key Vault keys (B) still require rotation and network exposure. Self-hosted (C) adds infrastructure complexity and cost without compliance benefits.

Reference: Managed identity
Extra: Security implementation: (1) Network: Create private endpoint → Links Azure OpenAI to VNet → Blocks public access → Approve connection, (2) Authentication: Enable managed identity on app (Functions, App Service) → Assign "Cognitive Services OpenAI User" role → SDK uses automatic token auth (no keys), (3) Data residency: Select region (Europe, US, Asia) → Data processed/stored in that region only, (4) Logging: Enable diagnostic settings → Send to Log Analytics → Exclude "prompt" and "completion" fields to redact PII (log metadata: timestamp, user, model, token count, latency), (5) Data processing: Azure OpenAI settings → Opt out of "Use data for model improvement" → Customer data not used for training. Key rotation: Not needed with managed identity (Azure AD handles automatically). Compliance certifications: SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA BAA, GDPR. Private endpoint cost: ~$7/mo per endpoint. Alternative: Service endpoints (less secure than private endpoints) restrict to Azure VNets.

Front:
An Azure AI solution architect must design capacity planning for Azure OpenAI supporting 10,000 users generating average 50 API calls/day with 500 tokens per call. Requirements: 99.9% uptime SLA, <3 second P95 latency for 90% of requests, support 2x traffic spikes during business hours, and minimize monthly costs.

A) Deploy Standard deployment with rate limits, monitor token usage, implement retry logic with exponential backoff, use Azure Front Door for multi-region failover
B) Purchase Provisioned Throughput Units (PTU) with reserved capacity
C) Deploy multiple Standard deployments with manual load balancing
Back:
A) Deploy Standard deployment with rate limits, monitor token usage, implement retry logic with exponential backoff, use Azure Front Door for multi-region failover

Capacity planning: 10k users × 50 calls/day × 500 tokens = 250M tokens/month. Standard deployment: Pay-per-token ($0.002/1k tokens = $500/mo), auto-scales to demand, no upfront commitment. PTU (B) requires minimum 50k TPM reserved capacity ($36k/mo) not justified for variable load. Multi-region failover via Front Door provides 99.9% SLA. Manual load balancing (C) adds operational complexity.

Reference: Deployment types
Extra: Capacity planning: (1) Calculate TPM: 10k users × 50 calls/day ÷ 1440 min = ~347 calls/min × 500 tokens = 173k TPM average; 2x spike = 346k TPM peak, (2) Deployment choice: Standard for variable workloads (<400k sustained TPM), PTU for predictable high volume (>400k sustained), (3) Rate limits: Standard tier default 240k TPM (sufficient for average, may throttle on spikes), (4) Retry strategy: Implement exponential backoff for 429 errors (rate limit exceeded), (5) Multi-region: Deploy to 2 regions (primary + failover) → Front Door health probes → auto-failover on primary failure → 99.9% SLA. Cost optimization: Standard pay-per-token vs PTU upfront commitment; Standard wins when daily variance >3x. Monitoring: Track P95 latency, throttle rate (429 responses), token consumption trends. Scaling: Standard auto-scales; if consistent throttling, consider PTU or request quota increase.

Front:
A company deploys Azure OpenAI models for customer support across EU and US regions. EU customer data must remain in EU due to GDPR. The solution must route requests to appropriate regional endpoints with automatic failover.

A) Azure OpenAI with Traffic Manager and regional deployments
B) Azure Front Door with custom routing rules and regional Azure OpenAI instances
C) Azure Application Gateway with geo-filtering and single global Azure OpenAI instance
Back:
B) Azure Front Door with custom routing rules and regional Azure OpenAI instances

Reference: Azure OpenAI deployment types
Extra: Front Door provides geo-routing, WAF, and automatic failover between regional endpoints while respecting data residency. Traffic Manager lacks WAF and application-layer routing. Application Gateway with a single global instance violates data residency requirements. Regional deployments are essential for GDPR compliance.

Front:
An AI application using GPT-4 experiences high costs from repetitive queries. 60% of requests are identical within a 24-hour period. The solution must reduce token consumption while maintaining response quality.

A) Implement semantic caching with embeddings-based similarity matching
B) Store exact query matches in Azure Cache for Redis with 24-hour TTL
C) Use Azure OpenAI fine-tuning to reduce response length
Back:
B) Store exact query matches in Azure Cache for Redis with 24-hour TTL

Reference: Monitor Azure OpenAI
Extra: For 60% identical requests, exact match caching (Redis) is simpler and more reliable than semantic similarity. Fine-tuning doesn't reduce token consumption for identical inputs. Semantic caching adds complexity and embedding costs; reserve it for near-duplicate queries. Exact caching provides 100% cache hit rate for duplicates.

Front:
A document processing pipeline extracts custom entities from legal contracts using Language service custom NER. The model must be versioned, A/B tested in production, and rolled back if F1-score drops below 0.85.

A) Deploy multiple model versions to separate Language resource endpoints with traffic splitting via API Management
B) Use Azure Machine Learning endpoints with blue-green deployment and model monitoring
C) Deploy model versions as separate CLU projects and route requests based on client headers
Back:
A) Deploy multiple model versions to separate Language resource endpoints with traffic splitting via API Management

Reference: Deploy custom NER models
Extra: Language service doesn't natively support blue-green deployment; APIM provides traffic splitting for A/B testing. Azure ML endpoints are for custom ML models, not Language service. CLU projects are for conversational understanding, not custom NER. APIM enables gradual rollout with performance monitoring and instant rollback.

Front:
A speech transcription service processes 10-hour conference recordings. Transcription must include speaker identification, timestamps, and support for 200+ concurrent batch jobs with 7-day retention.

A) Speech SDK real-time API with diarization and conversation transcription
B) Batch transcription API with speaker diarization and webhook callbacks
C) Speech-to-text containers with custom speaker recognition models
Back:
B) Batch transcription API with speaker diarization and webhook callbacks

Reference: Batch transcription
Extra: Batch API is designed for long-form audio processing with diarization, supports massive concurrency, and uses webhook callbacks for completion. Real-time API has session limits unsuitable for 10-hour files. Containers add operational complexity without batch processing benefits. Batch API provides built-in storage, queueing, and progress tracking.

Front:
An application monitors Azure OpenAI API calls for prompt injection attacks, jailbreak attempts, and data exfiltration patterns. Suspicious requests must be blocked before reaching the model.

A) Azure OpenAI content filters with custom blocklists and severity thresholds
B) Azure AI Content Safety prompt shields with user/document attack detection
C) Application Insights with custom KQL queries and alert rules
Back:
B) Azure AI Content Safety prompt shields with user/document attack detection

Reference: Jailbreak detection
Extra: Prompt shields specifically detect prompt injection, jailbreak, and indirect attacks in user inputs and documents. Content filters handle output safety (hate, violence), not input attacks. Application Insights provides telemetry, not pre-request blocking. Prompt shields analyze input patterns before LLM processing to prevent attacks.

Front:
A retail company builds a visual search feature where users upload product photos to find similar items. The catalog has 5 million products with 10 images each. Search latency must be under 500ms.

A) Computer Vision Image Analysis 4.0 with Florence embeddings and Azure AI Search vector index
B) Custom Vision object detection with SQL Database and full-text search
C) Azure OpenAI GPT-4V with image understanding and text-based product matching
Back:
A) Computer Vision Image Analysis 4.0 with Florence embeddings and Azure AI Search vector index

Reference: Image retrieval
Extra: Florence embeddings from Image Analysis 4.0 provide state-of-the-art visual similarity vectors optimized for retrieval. AI Search vector index enables sub-500ms queries at scale. Custom Vision is for classification/detection, not similarity search. GPT-4V is expensive per-query and slower than specialized embedding models for this use case.

Front:
A knowledge mining pipeline processes 500,000 PDF documents. The indexer must extract tables, key-value pairs, and custom domain entities, then project results into table storage for downstream analytics.

A) Built-in OCR skill + Entity Recognition skill + Custom skill for domain entities + Knowledge store table projection
B) Document Intelligence prebuilt read model + Custom Document Intelligence model + Shaper skill + Knowledge store object projection
C) Form Recognizer skill + Entity Linking skill + Knowledge store file projection
Back:
B) Document Intelligence prebuilt read model + Custom Document Intelligence model + Shaper skill + Knowledge store object projection

Reference: Knowledge store projections
Extra: Document Intelligence (Form Recognizer) excels at extracting tables and key-value pairs. Custom models handle domain entities. Shaper skill normalizes output for knowledge store. Object projection maps to table storage. Built-in OCR lacks table/KV extraction. Entity Linking finds Wikipedia entities, not custom domain entities. File projection stores blobs, not tabular data.

Front:
A conversational AI agent must call external APIs to check inventory, book appointments, and process payments based on user intent. The solution must handle multi-turn conversations with context and support parallel function execution.

A) Azure OpenAI GPT-4 with function calling and conversation history management
B) Conversational Language Understanding with external API integration via Logic Apps
C) Bot Framework Composer with Adaptive Cards and OAuth prompts
Back:
A) Azure OpenAI GPT-4 with function calling and conversation history management

Reference: Function calling
Extra: GPT-4 function calling enables the model to intelligently select and execute functions based on natural language, maintain conversation context, and handle parallel calls (e.g., check inventory while booking). CLU extracts intent/entities but doesn't orchestrate multi-step API workflows. Bot Framework requires explicit dialog management; GPT-4 handles orchestration naturally.

Front:
A video analytics solution processes security camera feeds to detect safety violations (no hard hat, restricted area entry). The system must run inference on 50 camera streams with real-time alerts and minimize cloud egress costs.

A) Deploy Custom Vision model to Azure IoT Edge devices with local inference and event-driven alerts
B) Stream video to Azure Spatial Analysis for person detection with custom zones
C) Use Video Indexer with custom models and webhook notifications
Back:
A) Deploy Custom Vision model to Azure IoT Edge devices with local inference and event-driven alerts

Reference: Export Custom Vision models
Extra: Edge deployment eliminates continuous video streaming costs (50 cameras = high egress), enables real-time processing, and maintains operation during connectivity loss. Spatial Analysis requires cloud connectivity and is designed for retail analytics, not custom safety violations. Video Indexer is for batch video understanding, not real-time stream analysis.

Front:
An AI search solution must rank documents by combining keyword relevance, semantic similarity, and user behavior signals (clicks, dwell time). Results must be personalized per-user and support A/B testing of ranking algorithms.

A) Azure AI Search with semantic ranking, scoring profiles, and custom scoring functions
B) Azure AI Search hybrid search with vector similarity and BM25, using Azure ML for personalization models
C) Cosmos DB vector search with pre-computed user embeddings and cosine similarity
Back:
B) Azure AI Search hybrid search with vector similarity and BM25, using Azure ML for personalization models

Reference: Hybrid search ranking
Extra: Hybrid search combines keyword (BM25) and vector similarity. Azure ML models personalization based on user behavior, feeding scores into AI Search. Semantic ranking improves relevance but doesn't incorporate user signals or enable A/B testing of custom models. Cosmos DB lacks AI Search's ranking tuning, filtering, and faceting capabilities.

Front:
A speech application must transcribe medical consultations with custom medical terminology, recognize 5 distinct speakers, and redact patient identifiers (names, MRN) from transcripts before storage.

A) Speech-to-text with custom speech model + conversation transcription + Text Analytics PII detection and redaction
B) Speech SDK real-time recognition + Azure OpenAI for terminology + manual speaker tracking + custom regex
C) Batch transcription with pronunciation lexicon + Azure AI Search entity recognition + blob lifecycle policies
Back:
A) Speech-to-text with custom speech model + conversation transcription + Text Analytics PII detection and redaction

Reference: Conversation transcription
Extra: Custom speech model adapts to medical terminology. Conversation transcription provides multi-speaker diarization (up to 8 speakers). Text Analytics PII detection identifies and redacts healthcare-specific identifiers per HIPAA. OpenAI isn't optimized for streaming transcription. Regex can't reliably detect all PII variants. AI Search is for indexing, not PII redaction.

Front:
A document translation pipeline must translate 10,000 legal contracts (50 pages each) from English to 15 languages while preserving formatting, maintaining glossary consistency, and tracking translation quality per document.

A) Document Translation batch API with custom glossaries, format preservation, and operation status polling
B) Translator Text API with document chunking, custom dictionary, and cosmos DB for tracking
C) Azure OpenAI GPT-4 with prompt-based translation and page-by-page processing
Back:
A) Document Translation batch API with custom glossaries, format preservation, and operation status polling

Reference: Document Translation
Extra: Document Translation is purpose-built for large-scale document translation with native format preservation (DOCX, PDF), custom glossaries, and batch processing. Text API requires manual chunking and format handling. GPT-4 is expensive for 500,000 pages (10K × 50), lacks glossary enforcement, and isn't optimized for production translation workloads.

Front:
An Azure OpenAI application experiences regional outages. The solution must automatically fail over to a secondary region with zero data loss, maintain conversation history consistency, and alert operations within 2 minutes.

A) Deploy Azure OpenAI in paired regions with Traffic Manager priority routing and Application Insights availability tests
B) Use Azure Front Door with health probes, origin groups spanning regions, and stateful conversation storage in Cosmos DB with multi-region writes
C) Implement client-side retry logic with exponential backoff and Azure Service Health alerts
Back:
B) Use Azure Front Door with health probes, origin groups spanning regions, and stateful conversation storage in Cosmos DB with multi-region writes

Reference: Azure OpenAI baseline architecture
Extra: Front Door provides automatic health-based failover (<10s detection), origin groups for regional endpoints, and built-in DDoS protection. Cosmos DB multi-region writes ensure conversation history consistency across regions. Traffic Manager has slower health probe intervals (30s+). Client-side retry doesn't handle regional outages gracefully. Front Door + Cosmos DB provides infrastructure-level HA.

Front:
A medical imaging AI must explain predictions (tumor detection) to radiologists by highlighting image regions contributing to the decision. The solution must comply with regulatory requirements for model transparency.

A) Use Attention Rollout or Grad-CAM with Custom Vision to generate saliency maps
B) Fine-tune GPT-4V with medical images and generate text explanations of predictions
C) Train a Custom Vision model and use the model explainability API for feature importance
Back:
A) Use Attention Rollout or Grad-CAM with Custom Vision to generate saliency maps

Reference: Custom Vision explainability
Extra: Saliency maps (Grad-CAM, attention rollout) provide visual explanations by highlighting image regions influencing predictions, meeting regulatory transparency requirements. GPT-4V generates text explanations but lacks pixel-level attribution. Custom Vision doesn't have a native "explainability API"; saliency visualization requires integration with explainability libraries. Visual attribution is essential for medical imaging trust.

Front:
A retail chatbot must answer questions using product manuals (500 PDFs), inventory database (SQL), and real-time pricing API. Responses must cite sources with page numbers and minimize hallucination.

A) Azure OpenAI with grounding on Azure AI Search index (PDFs + SQL), function calling for pricing API, and citation retrieval
B) Conversational Language Understanding with question answering over knowledge base and Logic Apps for API orchestration
C) Azure OpenAI fine-tuned model with product data embedded in training set and prompt engineering
Back:
A) Azure OpenAI with grounding on Azure AI Search index (PDFs + SQL), function calling for pricing API, and citation retrieval

Reference: Azure OpenAI on your data
Extra: Azure OpenAI on your data grounds responses in indexed content (PDFs, SQL via AI Search), provides automatic citations with page numbers, and reduces hallucination. Function calling integrates real-time APIs. CLU question answering lacks LLM reasoning and multi-source grounding. Fine-tuning embeds static knowledge but can't reference live inventory or provide citations.

Front:
A multilingual contact center routes calls based on detected language and transcribes conversations. The system must handle 40 languages, identify language within 3 seconds, and support real-time transcription with low latency.

A) Speech-to-text with automatic language detection, continuous recognition, and multi-language support
B) Azure OpenAI Whisper API with language parameter and streaming transcription
C) Translator Speech API with speech recognition and language identification
Back:
A) Speech-to-text with automatic language detection, continuous recognition, and multi-language support

Reference: Language identification
Extra: Speech service provides automatic language detection within 3-5 seconds, supports 40+ languages in continuous recognition mode with streaming results optimized for real-time scenarios. Whisper API is optimized for audio files, not real-time streaming. Translator Speech API was deprecated; Translator is text-only. Speech SDK's continuous recognition mode minimizes latency.

Front:
A news aggregation app must classify articles into 50 custom categories with 95%+ accuracy. The training set has 100,000 labeled articles. The solution must support model updates without service downtime.

A) Azure AI Language custom text classification with single-label classification, model versioning, and swap deployment
B) Azure Machine Learning text classification with AutoML and blue-green deployment to managed endpoints
C) Azure OpenAI GPT-4 with few-shot prompting and prompt versioning in App Configuration
Back:
A) Azure AI Language custom text classification with single-label classification, model versioning, and swap deployment

Reference: Custom text classification
Extra: Language service custom classification is optimized for this scenario: single/multi-label classification, supports 100K+ training samples, provides model deployment versioning with instant swaps (zero downtime). AutoML is more complex and costly for this use case. GPT-4 few-shot reaches token limits with 50 categories and lacks fine-tuning for precise classification at scale.

Front:
A video content platform must detect faces, index celebrities, identify products/brands, and extract spoken content with timestamps from 50TB of archived videos. Processing must complete within 1 week.

A) Video Indexer with face detection, celebrity recognition, OCR, brands detection, and speech-to-text indexing using batch processing
B) Custom Vision + Speech SDK + Computer Vision with Azure Batch for parallel processing and custom orchestration
C) Azure Media Services with Azure AI enrichment and custom skillsets
Back:
A) Video Indexer with face detection, celebrity recognition, OCR, brands detection, and speech-to-text indexing using batch processing

Reference: Video Indexer overview
Extra: Video Indexer provides all-in-one video analysis: face detection, celebrity recognition, OCR, brand detection, speech transcription, all with timestamps and batch processing capabilities. Custom solution with multiple AI services requires complex orchestration. Media Services focuses on encoding/streaming, not AI insights. Video Indexer's integrated pipeline simplifies large-scale video understanding.

Front:
A document processing API must extract structured data from mixed document types (invoices, receipts, W-2 forms, driver licenses). Each document type has a different schema. The solution must achieve 98%+ accuracy and support custom fields.

A) Document Intelligence with prebuilt models for each document type and composed model approach
B) Computer Vision OCR + Azure OpenAI GPT-4 with structured output for schema extraction
C) Custom Document Intelligence models trained individually for each document type with manual routing logic
Back:
A) Document Intelligence with prebuilt models for each document type and composed model approach

Reference: Composed models
Extra: Composed models enable a single endpoint that automatically routes documents to specialized prebuilt models (invoice, receipt, W-2, ID) based on document type classification, achieving 98%+ accuracy. OCR + GPT-4 adds cost and latency without matching specialized model accuracy. Manual routing logic is error-prone; composed models handle classification automatically.

Front:
A customer service chatbot must evaluate conversation quality, measure response relevance, detect task completion, and assess customer satisfaction. Evaluation must run on 100K conversations monthly with automated quality scoring.

A) Azure OpenAI GPT-4 as a judge with custom evaluation prompts assessing relevance, coherence, groundedness, and satisfaction with batch processing
B) Text Analytics sentiment analysis + manual human review sampling for quality metrics
C) Application Insights custom metrics tracking conversation length, API latency, and error rates
Back:
A) Azure OpenAI GPT-4 as a judge with custom evaluation prompts assessing relevance, coherence, groundedness, and satisfaction with batch processing

Reference: Evaluation metrics
Extra: LLM-as-judge with GPT-4 enables scalable evaluation of conversation quality across multiple dimensions (relevance, coherence, groundedness) that correlate well with human judgment. Batch processing handles 100K conversations cost-effectively. Sentiment analysis alone doesn't measure relevance or task completion. Application Insights provides telemetry, not quality assessment. GPT-4 evaluation is the emerging standard for LLM application quality.

Front:
An AI search solution ingests data from SharePoint, Azure Blob, SQL Database, and Cosmos DB. Indexing must run incrementally every 15 minutes, detect deletions, and maintain high availability during index rebuilds.

A) Configure indexers with change detection policies, deletion detection, incremental processing, and index aliases for zero-downtime rebuilds
B) Schedule full indexer runs every 15 minutes with parallel execution and duplicate detection
C) Use Event Grid triggers for real-time indexing with custom change tracking and versioned indexes
Back:
A) Configure indexers with change detection policies, deletion detection, incremental processing, and index aliases for zero-downtime rebuilds

Reference: Index changed and deleted blobs
Extra: Indexers support built-in change detection (watermark columns, LastModified), deletion detection (soft delete, native change tracking), and incremental runs for efficiency. Index aliases enable swapping to rebuilt indexes with zero downtime. Full runs every 15 min are inefficient and costly. Event Grid requires custom infrastructure; indexers provide turnkey incremental indexing with HA support.

Front:
A document summarization API must generate abstractive summaries for legal contracts (50-100 pages) with controllable summary length (250-500 words) and emphasize risk clauses. Response time must be under 30 seconds.

A) Azure OpenAI GPT-4 with prompt engineering, system message to emphasize risks, and max_tokens parameter for length control
B) Azure AI Language extractive summarization with sentence ranking and custom regex for risk terms
C) Azure AI Language abstractive summarization with sentence count parameter and query-focused summarization
Back:
C) Azure AI Language abstractive summarization with sentence count parameter and query-focused summarization

Reference: Text summarization
Extra: Language abstractive summarization generates natural language summaries with controllable length (sentence count) and query focus ("risk clauses"). It's optimized for document processing latency (<30s for long docs). GPT-4 can work but token limits for 50-page contracts require chunking and orchestration. Extractive summarization selects existing sentences; it doesn't generate new text and may miss nuanced risk implications.

Front:
A manufacturing facility uses computer vision to detect product defects on an assembly line. The solution must process 100 images/second, run offline during network outages, update models weekly, and log telemetry to cloud when connected.

A) Deploy Custom Vision ONNX models to NVIDIA Jetson devices with Azure IoT Edge, edge ML module for inference, and offline buffering with periodic sync
B) Stream images to Azure Computer Vision API with Azure Functions processing and Azure Blob retry logic
C) Train Azure Machine Learning models deployed to Kubernetes at the edge with Azure Arc-enabled ML
Back:
A) Deploy Custom Vision ONNX models to NVIDIA Jetson devices with Azure IoT Edge, edge ML module for inference, and offline buffering with periodic sync

Reference: Export Custom Vision models
Extra: IoT Edge enables edge inference with offline operation (disconnected manufacturing scenarios), hardware acceleration (Jetson GPU), and automatic cloud sync when connected. ONNX export enables deployment to edge devices. Cloud APIs require constant connectivity (100 imgs/sec = high bandwidth/cost). Azure Arc-enabled ML adds complexity; IoT Edge provides turnkey edge AI deployment with OTA model updates.

Front:
A search solution must combine full-text search, vector similarity, and geographical filters (distance from user) to rank results. Query performance must remain under 100ms for 10M documents.

A) Azure AI Search with hybrid search (BM25 + vectors), geo-spatial filtering, and performance tier optimization (partitions, replicas)
B) Cosmos DB with full-text search, vector search, and geo-spatial queries using composite indexes
C) Azure OpenAI embeddings with Azure SQL Database vector extension and PostGIS for geo-queries
Back:
A) Azure AI Search with hybrid search (BM25 + vectors), geo-spatial filtering, and performance tier optimization (partitions, replicas)

Reference: Filters in Azure AI Search
Extra: AI Search integrates hybrid search (keyword + vector), built-in geo-spatial filtering (geo.distance), and scales to 10M+ documents with partitions/replicas maintaining sub-100ms latency. Cosmos DB vector search (preview) lacks mature full-text ranking. Azure SQL vector extension doesn't provide AI Search's relevance tuning, facets, or distributed query optimization. AI Search is purpose-built for this multi-modal search scenario.
